blocks.html: VI Fibred Categories and Descent

pages.title: Introduction
pages.html:
Introduction

Contrary to what had been announced in the introduction to the preceding exposé, it turned out to be impossible to carry out descent in the category of preschemes, even in particular cases, without having first developed with sufficient care the language of descent in general categories.

The notion of “descent” provides the general framework for all the procedures of “gluing” of objects, and consequently of “gluing” of categories. The most classical case of gluing is relative to the datum of a topological space \(X\) and of a covering of \(X\) by open sets \(X_i\); if one is given, for every \(i\), a fibre space (say) \(E_i\) over \(X_i\), and, for every pair \((i,j)\), an isomorphism \(f_{ji}\) of \(E_i|X_{ij}\) onto \(E_j|X_{ij}\) (where one sets \(X_{ij}=X_i\cap X_j\)), satisfying a well-known transitivity condition (which one writes in abbreviated form \(f_{kj}f_{ji}=f_{ki}\)), one knows that there exists a fibre space \(E\) over \(X\), defined up to isomorphism by the condition that one have isomorphisms \(f_i\colon E|X_i\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} E_i\), satisfying the relations \(f_{ji}=f_jf_i^{-1}\) (with the usual abuse of notation). Let \(X'\) be the sum space of the \(X_i\), which is thus a fibre space over \(X\) (i.e. equipped with a continuous map \(X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\)). One can interpret more concisely the datum of the \(E_i\) as a fibre space \(E'\) over \(X'\), and the datum of the \(f_{ji}\) as an isomorphism between the two inverse images (by the two canonical projections) \(E''_1\) and \(E''_2\) of \(E'\) onto \(X''=X'\times_X X'\), the gluing condition then being expressible as an identity between isomorphisms of fibre spaces \(E'''_1\) and \(E'''_3\) over the triple fibre product \(X'''=X'\times_X X'\times_X X'\) (where \(E'''_i\) denotes the inverse image of \(E'\) on \(X'''\) by the canonical projection of index \(i\)). The construction of \(E\), starting from \(E'\) and \(f\), is a typical case of a “descent” procedure. Moreover, starting from a fibre space \(E\) over \(X\), one says that \(X\) is “locally trivial”, with fibre \(F\), if there exists an open covering \((X_i)\) of \(X\) such that the \(E|X_i\) are isomorphic to \(F\times X_i\), or, what amounts to the same, such that the inverse image \(E'\) of \(E\) on \(X'=\coprod_i X_i\) is isomorphic to \(X'\times F\).

Thus, the notion of “gluing” of objects, like that of “localization” of a property, are tied to the study of certain types of “base change” \(X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\). In Algebraic Geometry, many other types of base change, and in particular the faithfully flat morphisms \(X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\), must be regarded as corresponding to a procedure of “localization” relative to the preschemes, or other objects, “over” \(X\). This type of localization is used quite as much as ordinary topological localization (which is, moreover, a particular case of it). It is likewise the case (to a lesser extent) in Analytic Geometry.

Most of the proofs, reducing to verifications, are omitted or merely sketched; where appropriate we make precise the less obvious diagrams which arise in a proof.

pages.title: Universes, categories, equivalence of categories
pages.html:
1 Universes, categories, equivalence of categories

To avoid certain logical difficulties, we shall admit here the notion of Universe, which is a set “large enough” that one does not leave it by the usual operations of set theory; an “axiom of Universes” guarantees that every object lies in a Universe. For details, see a book in preparation by C. Chevalley and the lecturer1. Thus, the symbol \(\mathbf{Ens}\) denotes, not the category of all sets (a notion which has no meaning), but the category of sets that lie in a given Universe (which we shall not make precise here in the notation). Likewise, \(\mathbf{Cat}\) will denote the category of categories lying in the Universe in question, the “morphisms” of an object \(X\) of \(\mathbf{Cat}\) into another one \(Y\) being by definition the functors from \(X\) to \(Y\).

If \(\mathscr{C}\) is a category, we denote by \(\operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{C})\) the set of objects of \(\mathscr{C}\), by \(\operatorname{Fl}(\mathscr{C})\) the set of arrows of \(\mathscr{C}\) (or morphisms of \(\mathscr{C}\)). We shall therefore write \(X\in\operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{C})\), avoiding the current abuse of notation \(X\in\mathscr{C}\). If \(\mathscr{C}\) and \(\mathscr{C}'\) are two categories, a functor from \(\mathscr{C}\) to \(\mathscr{C}'\) will always be what is commonly called a covariant functor from \(\mathscr{C}\) to \(\mathscr{C}'\); to give it implies giving the target category and the source category, \(\mathscr{C}\) and \(\mathscr{C}'\). The functors from \(\mathscr{C}\) to \(\mathscr{C}'\) form a set, denoted \(\operatorname{Hom}(\mathscr{C},\mathscr{C}')\), which is the set of objects of a category denoted \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}(\mathscr{C},\mathscr{C}')\). By definition, a contravariant functor from \(\mathscr{C}\) to \(\mathscr{C}'\) is a functor from the opposite category \(\mathscr{C}^\circ\) of \(\mathscr{C}\) to \(\mathscr{C}'\).

We shall admit the notion of inverse limit and of direct limit of a functor \(F\colon \mathscr{I}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{C}\), and in particular the most common special cases of this notion: cartesian products and fibre products, notions dual to direct sums and amalgamated sums, and the usual formal properties of these operations.

For example, in the category \(\mathbf{Cat}\) introduced above, the inverse limits (relative to categories \(\mathscr{I}\) lying in the chosen Universe) exist; the set of objects (resp. the set of arrows) of the inverse limit category \(\mathscr{C}\) of the \(\mathscr{C}_i\) is obtained by taking the inverse limit of the sets of objects (resp. of the sets of arrows) of the categories \(\mathscr{C}_i\). The best-known case is that of the product of a family of categories. In what follows we shall constantly use the fibre product of two categories over a third one.

For everything concerning categories and functors, while awaiting the book in preparation already mentioned, see [1] (which is necessarily quite incomplete, even as regards the generalities sketched in the present section).

Let us take this occasion to make explicit the notion of equivalence of categories, which is not presented in a satisfactory way in [1]. A functor \(F\colon\mathscr{C}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{C}'\) is said to be faithful (resp. fully faithful) if for every pair of objects \(S\), \(T\) of \(\mathscr{C}\), the map \(u\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} F(u)\) from \(\operatorname{Hom}(S,T)\) to \(\operatorname{Hom}(F(S),F(T))\) is injective (resp. bijective). One says that \(F\) is an equivalence of categories if \(F\) is fully faithful, and if moreover every object \(S'\) of \(\mathscr{C}'\) is isomorphic to an object of the form \(F(S)\). One shows that this amounts to the same as saying that there exists a functor \(G\) from \(\mathscr{C}'\) to \(\mathscr{C}\) quasi-inverse to \(F\), i.e., such that \(GF\) is isomorphic to \(\mathrm{id}_{\mathscr{C}}\). When this is so, giving a functor \(G\colon\mathscr{C}'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{C}\) and an isomorphism \(\varphi\colon GF\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{id}_{\mathscr{C}'}\) is equivalent to giving, for every \(S'\in\operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{C}')\), a pair \((S,u)\) consisting of an object \(S\) of \(\mathscr{C}\) and an isomorphism \(u\colon F(S)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S'\), namely \((G(S),\varphi(S))\). (With these notations, there exists a unique functor \(\mathscr{C}'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{C}\) having the given map \(S\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} G(S)\) as its object-map, and such that the map \(S\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \varphi(S)\) is a homomorphism of functors \(FG\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{id}_{\mathscr{C}'}\)). Finally, if \(G\) is a functor quasi-inverse to \(F\), and if one chooses isomorphisms \(\varphi\colon FG\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow}\mathrm{id}_{\mathscr{C}'}\) and \(\psi\colon GF\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow}\mathrm{id}_{\mathscr{C}}\), then the two compatibility conditions on \(\varphi\), \(\psi\) stated in [1] are in fact equivalent to one another, and for every chosen isomorphism \(\varphi\), there exists a unique isomorphism \(\psi\) such that the said conditions are satisfied.
