pages.title: Categories over another
pages.html:
2 Categories over another

Let \(\mathscr{E}\) be a category in \(\mathbf{Univ}\); it is thus an object of \(\mathbf{Cat}\), and we may consider the category \(\mathbf{Cat}_{/\mathscr{E}}\) of the “objects of \(\mathbf{Cat}\) over \(\mathscr{E}\)”. An object of this category is thus a functor

\[ p\colon \mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{E} \]

One also says that the category \(\mathscr{F}\), equipped with such a functor, is a category over \(\mathscr{E}\), or an \(\mathscr{E}\)-category. One will thus call an \(\mathscr{E}\)-functor from a category \(\mathscr{F}\) over \(\mathscr{E}\) into a category \(\mathscr{G}\) over \(\mathscr{E}\), a functor

\[ f\colon\mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{G} \]

such that

\[ qf=p \]

where \(p\) and \(q\) are the projection-functors for \(\mathscr{F}\) resp. \(\mathscr{G}\). The set of \(\mathscr{E}\)-functors \(f\) from \(\mathscr{F}\) into \(\mathscr{G}\) is thus in one-to-one correspondence with the set of arrows with origin \(\mathscr{F}\) and target \(\mathscr{G}\) in \(\mathbf{Cat}_{/\mathscr{E}}\), without however this being an identity (since the datum of an \(f\) as above does not determine \(\mathscr{F}\) and \(\mathscr{G}\) as categories over \(\mathscr{E}\)); but of course, as in any other category \(\mathscr{C}_{/S}\), we shall currently make the abuse of language consisting in identifying the \(\mathscr{E}\)-functors (in the sense made explicit above) with arrows in a category \(\mathbf{Cat}_{/\mathscr{E}}\).

We shall denote by

\[ \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{E}}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G}) \]

the set of \(\mathscr{E}\)-functors from \(\mathscr{F}\) into \(\mathscr{G}\). Of course, a composite of \(\mathscr{E}\)-functors is an \(\mathscr{E}\)-functor (the composition in question corresponding by definition to the composition of arrows in \(\mathbf{Cat}_{/\mathscr{E}}\)).

Let us now consider two \(\mathscr{E}\)-functors

\[ f,g\colon \mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{G} \]

and a homomorphism of functors:

\[ u\colon f\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} g \]

We say that \(u\) is an \(\mathscr{E}\)-homomorphism or a “ homomorphism of \(\mathscr{E}\)-functors ”, if for every \(\xi\in\operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{F})\), we have

\[ q(u(\xi))=\mathrm{id}_{p(\xi)} \text{,} \]

in words: setting \(S=p(\xi)=qf(\xi)=qg(\xi)\in\operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{E})\), the morphism

\[ u(\xi)\colon f(\xi)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} g(\xi) \]

in \(\mathscr{G}\) is an \(\mathrm{id}_S\)-morphism. (In a general way, for every morphism \(\alpha\colon T \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) in \(\mathscr{E}\), and every category \(\mathscr{G}\) over \(\mathscr{E}\), a morphism \(v\) in \(\mathscr{G}\) is called an \(\alpha\)-morphism if \(q(v)=\alpha\), \(q\) denoting the projection functor \(\mathscr{G}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{E}\)). If we have a third \(\mathscr{E}\)-functor \(h\colon \mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{G}\) and an \(\mathscr{E}\)-homomorphism \(v\colon g\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} h\), then \(vu\) is likewise an \(\mathscr{E}\)-homomorphism. Thus, the \(\mathscr{E}\)-functors from \(\mathscr{F}\) into \(\mathscr{G}\), and the \(\mathscr{E}\)-homomorphisms of such, form a subcategory of the category \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G})\) of all functors from \(\mathscr{F}\) into \(\mathscr{G}\), which we shall call the category of \(\mathscr{E}\)-functors from \(\mathscr{F}\) into \(\mathscr{G}\) and which we shall denote by

\[ \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G}) \]

It is also the kernel subcategory of the pair of functors

\[ \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{R,S\colon\mathbf{Hom}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G})}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{\mathbf{Hom}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{E})}}\text{,} \]

where \(R\) is the constant functor defined by the object \(p\) of \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{E})\), and where \(S\) is the functor \(f\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} q\circ f\) defined by \(q\colon\mathscr{G}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{E}\).

To conclude these generalities, it remains to define the natural pairings between the categories \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G})\) by composition of \(\mathscr{E}\)-functors. In other words, we want to define a “composition functor”:

\[ \tag{i} {\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G})\times \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{G},\mathscr{H})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{H})} \]

when \(\mathscr{F}\), \(\mathscr{G}\), \(\mathscr{H}\) are three categories over \(\mathscr{E}\), in such a way that this functor induces, for the objects, the composition map \((f,g)\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} gf\) of \(\mathscr{E}\)-functors \(f\colon\mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{G}\) and \(g\colon \mathscr{G}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{H}\). For this, let us recall that one defines a canonical functor:

\[ \tag{ii} {\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G})\times\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}(\mathscr{G},\mathscr{H})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{H})} \]

which, for the objects, is none other than the composition map \((f,g)\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} gf\) of functors, and which transforms an arrow \((u,v)\), where

\[ u\colon f\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} f',\quad v\colon g\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} g' \]

are arrows in \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G})\) resp. in \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}(\mathscr{G},\mathscr{H})\), into the arrow

\[ v*u\colon gf\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} g'f' \]

defined by the relation:

\[ v*u(\xi)=v(f'(\xi))\cdot g(u(\xi))=g'(u(\xi))\cdot v(f(\xi)) \]

It is well known that one indeed obtains in this way a homomorphism from \(gf\) into \(g'f'\), and that (for \(f,g\) and \(u,v\) variable) one obtains in this way a functor (ii), i.e. that we have

\[ \tag{I} {\mathrm{id}_g*\mathrm{id}_f=\mathrm{id}_{gf},} \]
\[ \tag{II} {(v'*u')\circ(v*u)=(v'\circ v)*(u'\circ u)} \]

Let us also recall that we have an associativity formula for the canonical pairings (ii), which is expressed on the one hand by the associativity \((hg)f=h(gf)\) of the composition of functors, and on the other hand by the formula

\[ \tag{III} {(w*v)*u=w*(v*u)} \]

for the composition products of homomorphisms of functors (where \(u\colon f\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} f'\) and \(v\colon g\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} g'\) are as above, and where one supposes given moreover a homomorphism \(w\colon h\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} h'\) of functors \(h,h'\colon \mathscr{H}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{K}\)). I now claim that when \(\mathscr{F}\), \(\mathscr{G}\) are \(\mathscr{E}\)-categories, the canonical composition functor (ii) induces a functor (i). Since we already know that the composite of two \(\mathscr{E}\)-functors is an \(\mathscr{E}\)-functor, this amounts to saying that when \(u\colon f\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} f'\) and \(v\colon g\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} g'\) are homomorphisms of \(\mathscr{E}\)-functors, then \(v*u\colon gf\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} g'f'\) is likewise a homomorphism of \(\mathscr{E}\)-functors. This indeed follows trivially from the definitions. Since the pairings (i) are induced by the pairings (ii), they satisfy the same associativity property, expressed also in the formulas \((hg)f=h(gf)\) and \((w*v)*u=w*(v*u)\) for \(\mathscr{E}\)-functors and \(\mathscr{E}\)-homomorphisms of \(\mathscr{E}\)-functors.

To complete the formulary (I), (II), (III), let us also recall the formulas:

\[ \tag{IV} {v*\mathrm{id}_{\mathscr{F}}=v\quad\text{and}\quad\mathrm{id}_{\mathscr{G}}*u=u}, \]

where for simplicity one writes \(v*f\) or \(u*g\) in place of \(v*u\), when \(u\) resp. \(v\) is the identity automorphism of \(f\) resp. \(g\).

From the definition of the pairings (i) it follows that \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G})\) is a functor in \(\mathscr{F}\), \(\mathscr{G}\), from the product category \({\mathbf{Cat}_{/\mathscr{E}}}^\circ\times\mathbf{Cat}_{/\mathscr{E}}\) to the category \(\mathbf{Cat}\). For indeed if \(g\colon \mathscr{G}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{G}_1\) is an \(\mathscr{E}\)-functor, i.e. an object of \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{G},\mathscr{G}_1)\), then setting in (i) \(\mathscr{H}=\mathscr{G}_1\), there corresponds to it a functor

\[ g_*\colon\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G}_1) \]

One defines in the analogous way, for an \(\mathscr{E}\)-functor \(f\colon \mathscr{F}_1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{F}\), a functor

\[ f^*:\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{F}_1,\mathscr{G}) \]

To abbreviate, one also denotes these functors by the symbols \(f\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} g\circ f\) resp. \(g\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} g\circ f\) (which denote only, in fact, the corresponding maps on the sets of objects). It follows from the associativity property indicated above that in this way one indeed obtains, as announced, a functor \(\mathbf{Cat}_{/\mathscr{E}}^\circ\times\mathbf{Cat}_{/\mathscr{E}}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathbf{Cat}\).

blocks.html: 2 Categories over another

Let \(\mathscr{E}\) be a category in \(\mathbf{Univ}\); it is thus an object of \(\mathbf{Cat}\), and we may consider the category \(\mathbf{Cat}_{/\mathscr{E}}\) of the “objects of \(\mathbf{Cat}\) over \(\mathscr{E}\)”. An object of this category is thus a functor
\[ p\colon \mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{E} \]

One also says that the category \(\mathscr{F}\), equipped with such a functor, is a category over \(\mathscr{E}\), or an \(\mathscr{E}\)-category. One will thus call an \(\mathscr{E}\)-functor from a category \(\mathscr{F}\) over \(\mathscr{E}\) into a category \(\mathscr{G}\) over \(\mathscr{E}\), a functor
\[ f\colon\mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{G} \]
such that
\[ qf=p \]

where \(p\) and \(q\) are the projection-functors for \(\mathscr{F}\) resp. \(\mathscr{G}\). The set of \(\mathscr{E}\)-functors \(f\) from \(\mathscr{F}\) into \(\mathscr{G}\) is thus in one-to-one correspondence with the set of arrows with origin \(\mathscr{F}\) and target \(\mathscr{G}\) in \(\mathbf{Cat}_{/\mathscr{E}}\), without however this being an identity (since the datum of an \(f\) as above does not determine \(\mathscr{F}\) and \(\mathscr{G}\) as categories over \(\mathscr{E}\)); but of course, as in any other category \(\mathscr{C}_{/S}\), we shall currently make the abuse of language consisting in identifying the \(\mathscr{E}\)-functors (in the sense made explicit above) with arrows in a category \(\mathbf{Cat}_{/\mathscr{E}}\).
We shall denote by
\[ \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{E}}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G}) \]

the set of \(\mathscr{E}\)-functors from \(\mathscr{F}\) into \(\mathscr{G}\). Of course, a composite of \(\mathscr{E}\)-functors is an \(\mathscr{E}\)-functor (the composition in question corresponding by definition to the composition of arrows in \(\mathbf{Cat}_{/\mathscr{E}}\)).
Let us now consider two \(\mathscr{E}\)-functors
\[ f,g\colon \mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{G} \]
and a homomorphism of functors:
\[ u\colon f\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} g \]
We say that \(u\) is an \(\mathscr{E}\)-homomorphism or a “ homomorphism of \(\mathscr{E}\)-functors ”, if for every \(\xi\in\operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{F})\), we have
\[ q(u(\xi))=\mathrm{id}_{p(\xi)} \text{,} \]
in words: setting \(S=p(\xi)=qf(\xi)=qg(\xi)\in\operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{E})\), the morphism
\[ u(\xi)\colon f(\xi)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} g(\xi) \]

in \(\mathscr{G}\) is an \(\mathrm{id}_S\)-morphism. (In a general way, for every morphism \(\alpha\colon T \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) in \(\mathscr{E}\), and every category \(\mathscr{G}\) over \(\mathscr{E}\), a morphism \(v\) in \(\mathscr{G}\) is called an \(\alpha\)-morphism if \(q(v)=\alpha\), \(q\) denoting the projection functor \(\mathscr{G}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{E}\)). If we have a third \(\mathscr{E}\)-functor \(h\colon \mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{G}\) and an \(\mathscr{E}\)-homomorphism \(v\colon g\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} h\), then \(vu\) is likewise an \(\mathscr{E}\)-homomorphism. Thus, the \(\mathscr{E}\)-functors from \(\mathscr{F}\) into \(\mathscr{G}\), and the \(\mathscr{E}\)-homomorphisms of such, form a subcategory of the category \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G})\) of all functors from \(\mathscr{F}\) into \(\mathscr{G}\), which we shall call the category of \(\mathscr{E}\)-functors from \(\mathscr{F}\) into \(\mathscr{G}\) and which we shall denote by
\[ \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G}) \]
It is also the kernel subcategory of the pair of functors
\[ \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{R,S\colon\mathbf{Hom}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G})}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{\mathbf{Hom}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{E})}}\text{,} \]

where \(R\) is the constant functor defined by the object \(p\) of \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{E})\), and where \(S\) is the functor \(f\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} q\circ f\) defined by \(q\colon\mathscr{G}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{E}\).

To conclude these generalities, it remains to define the natural pairings between the categories \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G})\) by composition of \(\mathscr{E}\)-functors. In other words, we want to define a “composition functor”:

blocks.label: i
blocks.html: \[ \tag{i} {\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G})\times \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{G},\mathscr{H})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{H})} \]

when \(\mathscr{F}\), \(\mathscr{G}\), \(\mathscr{H}\) are three categories over \(\mathscr{E}\), in such a way that this functor induces, for the objects, the composition map \((f,g)\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} gf\) of \(\mathscr{E}\)-functors \(f\colon\mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{G}\) and \(g\colon \mathscr{G}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{H}\). For this, let us recall that one defines a canonical functor:

blocks.label: ii
blocks.html: \[ \tag{ii} {\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G})\times\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}(\mathscr{G},\mathscr{H})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{H})} \]
which, for the objects, is none other than the composition map \((f,g)\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} gf\) of functors, and which transforms an arrow \((u,v)\), where
\[ u\colon f\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} f',\quad v\colon g\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} g' \]
are arrows in \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G})\) resp. in \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}(\mathscr{G},\mathscr{H})\), into the arrow
\[ v*u\colon gf\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} g'f' \]
defined by the relation:
\[ v*u(\xi)=v(f'(\xi))\cdot g(u(\xi))=g'(u(\xi))\cdot v(f(\xi)) \]
It is well known that one indeed obtains in this way a homomorphism from \(gf\) into \(g'f'\), and that (for \(f,g\) and \(u,v\) variable) one obtains in this way a functor (ii), i.e. that we have

blocks.label: I
blocks.html: \[ \tag{I} {\mathrm{id}_g*\mathrm{id}_f=\mathrm{id}_{gf},} \]

blocks.label: II
blocks.html: \[ \tag{II} {(v'*u')\circ(v*u)=(v'\circ v)*(u'\circ u)} \]

Let us also recall that we have an associativity formula for the canonical pairings (ii), which is expressed on the one hand by the associativity \((hg)f=h(gf)\) of the composition of functors, and on the other hand by the formula

blocks.label: III
blocks.html: \[ \tag{III} {(w*v)*u=w*(v*u)} \]

for the composition products of homomorphisms of functors (where \(u\colon f\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} f'\) and \(v\colon g\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} g'\) are as above, and where one supposes given moreover a homomorphism \(w\colon h\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} h'\) of functors \(h,h'\colon \mathscr{H}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{K}\)). I now claim that when \(\mathscr{F}\), \(\mathscr{G}\) are \(\mathscr{E}\)-categories, the canonical composition functor (ii) induces a functor (i). Since we already know that the composite of two \(\mathscr{E}\)-functors is an \(\mathscr{E}\)-functor, this amounts to saying that when \(u\colon f\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} f'\) and \(v\colon g\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} g'\) are homomorphisms of \(\mathscr{E}\)-functors, then \(v*u\colon gf\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} g'f'\) is likewise a homomorphism of \(\mathscr{E}\)-functors. This indeed follows trivially from the definitions. Since the pairings (i) are induced by the pairings (ii), they satisfy the same associativity property, expressed also in the formulas \((hg)f=h(gf)\) and \((w*v)*u=w*(v*u)\) for \(\mathscr{E}\)-functors and \(\mathscr{E}\)-homomorphisms of \(\mathscr{E}\)-functors.
To complete the formulary (I), (II), (III), let us also recall the formulas:

blocks.label: IV
blocks.html: \[ \tag{IV} {v*\mathrm{id}_{\mathscr{F}}=v\quad\text{and}\quad\mathrm{id}_{\mathscr{G}}*u=u}, \]
where for simplicity one writes \(v*f\) or \(u*g\) in place of \(v*u\), when \(u\) resp. \(v\) is the identity automorphism of \(f\) resp. \(g\).

From the definition of the pairings (i) it follows that \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G})\) is a functor in \(\mathscr{F}\), \(\mathscr{G}\), from the product category \({\mathbf{Cat}_{/\mathscr{E}}}^\circ\times\mathbf{Cat}_{/\mathscr{E}}\) to the category \(\mathbf{Cat}\). For indeed if \(g\colon \mathscr{G}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{G}_1\) is an \(\mathscr{E}\)-functor, i.e. an object of \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{G},\mathscr{G}_1)\), then setting in (i) \(\mathscr{H}=\mathscr{G}_1\), there corresponds to it a functor

\[ g_*\colon\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G}_1) \]
One defines in the analogous way, for an \(\mathscr{E}\)-functor \(f\colon \mathscr{F}_1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{F}\), a functor

\[ f^*:\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{F}_1,\mathscr{G}) \]

To abbreviate, one also denotes these functors by the symbols \(f\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} g\circ f\) resp. \(g\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} g\circ f\) (which denote only, in fact, the corresponding maps on the sets of objects). It follows from the associativity property indicated above that in this way one indeed obtains, as announced, a functor \(\mathbf{Cat}_{/\mathscr{E}}^\circ\times\mathbf{Cat}_{/\mathscr{E}}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathbf{Cat}\).

pages.title: Base change in the categories over \(\mathscr{E}\)
pages.html:
3 Base change in the categories over \(\mathscr{E}\)

Since in \(\mathbf{Cat}\) the inverse limits (relative to categories \(\mathscr{I}\) that are elements of \(\mathbf{Univ}\)) exist, the same holds in \(\mathbf{Cat}_{/\mathscr{E}}\); in particular cartesian products exist there, which are interpreted as fibre products in \(\mathbf{Cat}\). In accordance with the general notations, if \(\mathscr{F}\) and \(\mathscr{G}\) are categories over \(\mathscr{E}\), one denotes by

\[ \mathscr{F}\times_{\mathscr{E}}\mathscr{G} \]

their product in \(\mathbf{Cat}_{/\mathscr{E}}\), i.e. their fibre product over \(\mathscr{E}\) in \(\mathbf{Cat}\), regarded as a category over \(\mathscr{E}\). Thus, \(\mathscr{F}\times_{\mathscr{E}} \mathscr{G}\) is equipped with two \(\mathscr{E}\)-functors \(\mathrm{pr}_1\) and \(\mathrm{pr}_2\), which define, for every category \(\mathscr{H}\) over \(\mathscr{E}\), a bijection

\[ \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{E}}(\mathscr{H},\mathscr{F}\times_{\mathscr{E}}\mathscr{G})\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{E}}(\mathscr{H},\mathscr{F})\times\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{E}}(\mathscr{H},\mathscr{G}). \]

This bijection moreover comes from an isomorphism of categories

\[ \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{H},\mathscr{F}\times_{\mathscr{E}} \mathscr{G})\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow}\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{H},\mathscr{F})\times \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{H},\mathscr{G}) \]

by taking the sets of objects of the two sides, where the functor written is the one whose components are the functors \(h\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \mathrm{pr}_1\circ h\) and \(h\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \mathrm{pr}_2\circ h\) from the first side into the two factors of the second. We leave to the reader the care of verifying that one indeed obtains in this way an isomorphism (the analogous fact being true, more generally, whenever one has an inverse limit of categories — and not only in the case of a fibre product).

Let us moreover recall that one has (as was said in 1):

\begin{eqnarray*} \operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{F}\times_\mathscr{E}\mathscr{G})&=& \operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{F})\times_{\operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{E})}\operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{G})\\ \operatorname{Fl}(\mathscr{F}\times_\mathscr{E}\mathscr{G})&=& \operatorname{Fl}(\mathscr{F})\times_{\operatorname{Fl}(\mathscr{E})}\operatorname{Fl}(\mathscr{G}), \end{eqnarray*}

the composition of arrows being moreover done component by component.

In the sequel, we consider a functor

\[ \lambda\colon\mathscr{E}'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{E} \]

and for every category \(\mathscr{F}\) over \(\mathscr{E}\), one considers \(\mathscr{F}\times_\mathscr{E}\mathscr{E}'\) as a category over \(\mathscr{E}'\) by means of \(\mathrm{pr}_2\); in other words, we interpret the operation “fibre product” as an operation “base change”, the functor \(\lambda\colon\mathscr{E}'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{E}\) taking the name of “base change functor”. In accordance with the well-known general facts, one obtains in this way a functor, called the base change functor for \(\lambda\):

\[ \lambda^*\colon\mathbf{Cat}_{/\mathscr{E}}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathbf{Cat}_{/\mathscr{E}'}, \]

(adjoint of the functor “restriction of the base” which, to every category \(\mathscr{F}'\) over \(\mathscr{E}'\), with projection functor \(p'\), associates \(\mathscr{F}'\), regarded as a category over \(\mathscr{E}\) by the functor \(p=\lambda p'\)). As is well known in the general case of a base change functor in a category, the base change functor “commutes with inverse limits”, and in particular “transforms” fibre products over \(\mathscr{E}\) into fibre products over \(\mathscr{E}'\).

Let \(\mathscr{F}\) and \(\mathscr{G}\) be two categories over \(\mathscr{E}\); we are going to define a canonical isomorphism:

\begin{multline*} \tag{i} \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}'/-}(\mathscr{F}',\mathscr{G}')\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{F}\times_{\mathscr{E}}\mathscr{E}',\mathscr{G})\\ \text{where }\mathscr{F}'=\mathscr{F}\times_{\mathscr{E}}\mathscr{E}'\text{, } \mathscr{G}'=\mathscr{G}\times_{\mathscr{E}}\mathscr{E}'. \end{multline*}

For this, let us consider the functor

\[ \mathrm{pr}_1\colon\mathscr{G}'=\mathscr{G}\times_{\mathscr{E}}\mathscr{E}'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{G}, \]

and let us define (i) by

\[ F\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \mathrm{pr}_1\circ F, \]

which a priori denotes a functor

\[ \tag{ii} {\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}(\mathscr{F}',\mathscr{G}')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}(\mathscr{F}',\mathscr{G})} \]

It thus only remains to verify that this last one induces a functor for the subcategories (i), and that this last one is an isomorphism. That (ii) induces a bijection

\[ \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{E}'/-}(\mathscr{F}',\mathscr{G}')\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{F}\times_{\mathscr{E}}\mathscr{E}',\mathscr{G}) \]

is the characteristic property of the base change functor. It thus remains to prove that if \(F\), \(G\) are \(\mathscr{E}'\)-functors \(\mathscr{F}'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{G}'\), then the map

\[ u\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \mathrm{pr}_1\circ u \]

induces a bijection

\[ \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{E}'}(F,G)\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow}\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{E}}(\mathrm{pr}_1\circ F,\mathrm{pr}_1\circ G). \]

The verification of this fact is immediate, and left to the reader.

It follows from this isomorphism (i), and from the end of the preceding section, that

\[ \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}'/-}(\mathscr{F}\times_{\mathscr{E}}\mathscr{E}',\mathscr{G} \times_{\mathscr{E}}\mathscr{E}') \]

may be regarded as a functor in \(\mathscr{E}',\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G}\), from the category \(\mathbf{Cat}_{/\mathscr{E}}^\circ\times\mathbf{Cat}_{/\mathscr{E}}^\circ\times\mathbf{Cat}_{/\mathscr{E}}\) into the category \(\mathbf{Cat}\), isomorphic to the functor defined by the expression \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{F}\times_{\mathscr{E}}\mathscr{E}',\mathscr{G})\). In particular, for \(\mathscr{F}\), \(\mathscr{G}\) fixed, one obtains a functor in \(\mathscr{E}'\), \(\mathscr{E}'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}''/-}(\mathscr{F}',\mathscr{G}')= \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}'/-}(\mathscr{F}\times_{\mathscr{E}} \mathscr{E}',\mathscr{G}\times_{\mathscr{E}}\mathscr{E}')\), and in particular the \(\mathscr{E}\)-functor of projection \(\lambda\colon \mathscr{E}'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{E}\) defines a morphism, i.e. a functor

\[ \lambda^*_{\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G}}\colon \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}'/-}(\mathscr{F}',\mathscr{G}') \]

which we are going to make explicit. For the sets of objects of the two sides, it is the map

\[ f\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} f'=f\times_{\mathscr{E}}\mathscr{E}' \]

which expresses the functorial dependence of \(\mathscr{F}\times_{\mathscr{E}}\mathscr{E}'\) on the object \(\mathscr{F}\) over \(\mathscr{E}\). On the other hand, let us consider two \(\mathscr{E}\)-functors

\[ f,g\colon\mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{G} \]

and a homomorphism of \(\mathscr{E}\)-functors

\[ u\colon f\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} g, \]

we will make explicit the corresponding homomorphism of \(\mathscr{E}'\)-functors:

\[ u'\colon f'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} g'. \]

For every

\[ \xi'=(\xi,S')\in\operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{F}') \]

with

\[ \xi\in\operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{F}),\quad S'\in\operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{E}'),\quad p(\xi)=\lambda(S')=S \]

the morphism

\[ u'(\xi')\colon f'(\xi')=(f(\xi),S')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} g'(\xi')=(g(\xi),S')\quad \text{in } \mathscr{G}' \]

is defined by the formula

\[ u'(\xi')=(u(\xi),\mathrm{id}_{S'}) \]

(which is indeed an \(S'\)-morphism in \(\mathscr{G}'\), since \(q(u(\xi))=\lambda(\mathrm{id}_{S'})=\mathrm{id}_S\)).

Let us now consider an arbitrary \(\mathscr{E}\)-functor

\[ \lambda'\colon\mathscr{E}''\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{E}' \]

and the corresponding functor

\[ \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}'/-}(\mathscr{F}\times_{\mathscr{E}}\mathscr{E}', \mathscr{G}\times_{\mathscr{E}}\mathscr{E}')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}''/-}(\mathscr{F}\times_{\mathscr{E}}\mathscr{E}'', \mathscr{G}\times_{\mathscr{E}}\mathscr{E}''), \]

I claim that this functor is none other than the functor one obtains by the preceding procedure, starting from \(\mathscr{F}'\) and \(\mathscr{G}'\) over \(\mathscr{E}'\) and regarding \(\mathscr{E}''\) as a category over \(\mathscr{E}'\), taking into account the isomorphisms of « transitivity of base change »:

\[ \mathscr{F}'\times_{\mathscr{E}'}\mathscr{E}''\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \mathscr{F}''=\mathscr{F}\times_{\mathscr{E}}\mathscr{E}''\quad \text{and}\quad\mathscr{G}'\times_{\mathscr{E}'}\mathscr{E}''\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow}\mathscr{G}'' =\mathscr{G}\times_{\mathscr{E}}\mathscr{E}'', \]

which imply a canonical isomorphism

\[ \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}''/-}(\mathscr{F}'\times_{\mathscr{E}'}\mathscr{E}'', \mathscr{G}'\times_{\mathscr{E}'}\mathscr{E}'')\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}''/-}(\mathscr{F}\times_{\mathscr{E}}\mathscr{E}'', \mathscr{G}\times_{\mathscr{E}}\mathscr{E}'') \]

The verification of this compatibility is immediate, and left to the reader.

The functors we have just defined are compatible with the pairings defined in the preceding article; more precisely, if \(\mathscr{F}\), \(\mathscr{G}\), \(\mathscr{H}\) are categories over \(\mathscr{E}\) and if we set

\[ \mathscr{F}'=\mathscr{F}\times_{\mathscr{E}}\mathscr{E}',\quad \mathscr{G}'= \mathscr{G}\times_{\mathscr{E}}\mathscr{E}',\quad \mathscr{H}'= \mathscr{H}\times_{\mathscr{E}}\mathscr{E}', \]

we have commutativity in the following diagram of functors:

\[ \xymatrix{{\mathbf{Hom}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G})\times \mathbf{Hom}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{G},\mathscr{H})}\ar[r]\ar[d]_{\lambda^*_{\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G}}\times \lambda^*_{\mathscr{G},\mathscr{H}}}&{\mathbf{Hom}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{H})}\ar[d]^{\lambda^*_{\mathscr{F},\mathscr{H}}}\\{\mathbf{Hom}_{\mathscr{E}'/-}(\mathscr{F}',\mathscr{G}')\times \mathbf{Hom}_{\mathscr{E}'/-}(\mathscr{G}',\mathscr{H}')}\ar[r]&{\mathbf{Hom}_{\mathscr{E}'/-}(\mathscr{F}',\mathscr{H}')}} \]

where the horizontal arrows are the composition-functors defined in the preceding article. This commutativity is expressed by the formulas

\[ (gf)'=g'f' \]

for \(f\in\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{E}}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G})\), \(g\in\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{E}}(\mathscr{G},\mathscr{H})\), (a formula which simply expresses the functoriality of base change), and the formula

\[ (v*u)'=v'*u' \]

when \(u\colon f\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} f_1\) is an arrow of \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G})\) and \(v\colon g\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} g_1\) an arrow of \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{G},\mathscr{H})\). The verification of this formula follows easily from the definitions.

In what follows, we shall be interested above all in \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G})\) (and certain noteworthy subcategories of it) when \(\mathscr{F}=\mathscr{E}\), and for this reason we introduce a special notation:

\[ \mathbf{\Gamma}(\mathscr{G}/\mathscr{E})= \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}}(\mathscr{E},\mathscr{G}),\quad \Gamma(\mathscr{G}/\mathscr{E})=\operatorname{Ob}(\mathbf{\Gamma}(\mathscr{G}/\mathscr{E}))= \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{E}}(\mathscr{E},\mathscr{G}). \]

Remarks 3

When \(\mathscr{E}\) is a punctual category, i.e. \(\operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{E})\) and \(\operatorname{Fl}(\mathscr{E})\) reduced to a single element, which also means that \(\mathscr{E}\) is a final object of the category \(\mathbf{Cat}\), then the datum of a category over \(\mathscr{E}\) is equivalent to the datum of a category tout court, (since there will be a unique functor from \(\mathscr{F}\) to \(\mathscr{E}\)). More precisely, \(\mathbf{Cat}_{/\mathscr{E}}\) is then isomorphic to \(\mathbf{Cat}\). Moreover, the categories \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G})\) are then none other than the \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G})\). Let us then recall that the fundamental formula

\[ \operatorname{Hom}(\mathscr{H},\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G}))\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}(\mathscr{F}\times\mathscr{H},\mathscr{G}) \]

(an isomorphism functorial in the three arguments appearing in it), allows one to interpret \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G})\) axiomatically, in terms internal to the category \(\mathbf{Cat}\), so that the known formalism of the categories \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}\) appears as a particular case of a formalism valid in categories such as \(\mathbf{Cat}\), where « \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}\) objects » (defined by the preceding formula) exist. There is an analogous interpretation of \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G})\) when one again supposes \(\mathscr{E}\) arbitrary, by the formula

\[ \operatorname{Hom}(\mathscr{H},\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G}))\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{E}}(\mathscr{F}\times\mathscr{H},\mathscr{G}) \]

(an isomorphism functorial in the three arguments). In this way, the formal properties set forth in 2, 3 are particular cases of more general results, valid in categories where the objects \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G})\) (when \(\mathscr{F}\), \(\mathscr{G}\) are two objects of the category above a third \(\mathscr{E}\)) exist.

blocks.html: 3 Base change in the categories over \(\mathscr{E}\)

Since in \(\mathbf{Cat}\) the inverse limits (relative to categories \(\mathscr{I}\) that are elements of \(\mathbf{Univ}\)) exist, the same holds in \(\mathbf{Cat}_{/\mathscr{E}}\); in particular cartesian products exist there, which are interpreted as fibre products in \(\mathbf{Cat}\). In accordance with the general notations, if \(\mathscr{F}\) and \(\mathscr{G}\) are categories over \(\mathscr{E}\), one denotes by
\[ \mathscr{F}\times_{\mathscr{E}}\mathscr{G} \]

their product in \(\mathbf{Cat}_{/\mathscr{E}}\), i.e. their fibre product over \(\mathscr{E}\) in \(\mathbf{Cat}\), regarded as a category over \(\mathscr{E}\). Thus, \(\mathscr{F}\times_{\mathscr{E}} \mathscr{G}\) is equipped with two \(\mathscr{E}\)-functors \(\mathrm{pr}_1\) and \(\mathrm{pr}_2\), which define, for every category \(\mathscr{H}\) over \(\mathscr{E}\), a bijection

\[ \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{E}}(\mathscr{H},\mathscr{F}\times_{\mathscr{E}}\mathscr{G})\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{E}}(\mathscr{H},\mathscr{F})\times\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{E}}(\mathscr{H},\mathscr{G}). \]
This bijection moreover comes from an isomorphism of categories

\[ \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{H},\mathscr{F}\times_{\mathscr{E}} \mathscr{G})\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow}\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{H},\mathscr{F})\times \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{H},\mathscr{G}) \]

by taking the sets of objects of the two sides, where the functor written is the one whose components are the functors \(h\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \mathrm{pr}_1\circ h\) and \(h\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \mathrm{pr}_2\circ h\) from the first side into the two factors of the second. We leave to the reader the care of verifying that one indeed obtains in this way an isomorphism (the analogous fact being true, more generally, whenever one has an inverse limit of categories — and not only in the case of a fibre product).
Let us moreover recall that one has (as was said in 1):

\begin{eqnarray*} \operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{F}\times_\mathscr{E}\mathscr{G})&=& \operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{F})\times_{\operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{E})}\operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{G})\\ \operatorname{Fl}(\mathscr{F}\times_\mathscr{E}\mathscr{G})&=& \operatorname{Fl}(\mathscr{F})\times_{\operatorname{Fl}(\mathscr{E})}\operatorname{Fl}(\mathscr{G}), \end{eqnarray*}
the composition of arrows being moreover done component by component.
In the sequel, we consider a functor
\[ \lambda\colon\mathscr{E}'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{E} \]

and for every category \(\mathscr{F}\) over \(\mathscr{E}\), one considers \(\mathscr{F}\times_\mathscr{E}\mathscr{E}'\) as a category over \(\mathscr{E}'\) by means of \(\mathrm{pr}_2\); in other words, we interpret the operation “fibre product” as an operation “base change”, the functor \(\lambda\colon\mathscr{E}'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{E}\) taking the name of “base change functor”. In accordance with the well-known general facts, one obtains in this way a functor, called the base change functor for \(\lambda\):
\[ \lambda^*\colon\mathbf{Cat}_{/\mathscr{E}}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathbf{Cat}_{/\mathscr{E}'}, \]

(adjoint of the functor “restriction of the base” which, to every category \(\mathscr{F}'\) over \(\mathscr{E}'\), with projection functor \(p'\), associates \(\mathscr{F}'\), regarded as a category over \(\mathscr{E}\) by the functor \(p=\lambda p'\)). As is well known in the general case of a base change functor in a category, the base change functor “commutes with inverse limits”, and in particular “transforms” fibre products over \(\mathscr{E}\) into fibre products over \(\mathscr{E}'\).
Let \(\mathscr{F}\) and \(\mathscr{G}\) be two categories over \(\mathscr{E}\); we are going to define a canonical isomorphism:

blocks.label: i
blocks.html: \begin{multline*} \tag{i} \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}'/-}(\mathscr{F}',\mathscr{G}')\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{F}\times_{\mathscr{E}}\mathscr{E}',\mathscr{G})\\ \text{where }\mathscr{F}'=\mathscr{F}\times_{\mathscr{E}}\mathscr{E}'\text{, } \mathscr{G}'=\mathscr{G}\times_{\mathscr{E}}\mathscr{E}'. \end{multline*}
For this, let us consider the functor
\[ \mathrm{pr}_1\colon\mathscr{G}'=\mathscr{G}\times_{\mathscr{E}}\mathscr{E}'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{G}, \]
and let us define (i) by
\[ F\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \mathrm{pr}_1\circ F, \]
which a priori denotes a functor

blocks.label: ii
blocks.html: \[ \tag{ii} {\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}(\mathscr{F}',\mathscr{G}')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}(\mathscr{F}',\mathscr{G})} \]
It thus only remains to verify that this last one induces a functor for the subcategories (i), and that this last one is an isomorphism. That (ii) induces a bijection
\[ \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{E}'/-}(\mathscr{F}',\mathscr{G}')\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{F}\times_{\mathscr{E}}\mathscr{E}',\mathscr{G}) \]

is the characteristic property of the base change functor. It thus remains to prove that if \(F\), \(G\) are \(\mathscr{E}'\)-functors \(\mathscr{F}'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{G}'\), then the map
\[ u\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \mathrm{pr}_1\circ u \]
induces a bijection
\[ \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{E}'}(F,G)\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow}\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{E}}(\mathrm{pr}_1\circ F,\mathrm{pr}_1\circ G). \]
The verification of this fact is immediate, and left to the reader.
It follows from this isomorphism (i), and from the end of the preceding section, that
\[ \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}'/-}(\mathscr{F}\times_{\mathscr{E}}\mathscr{E}',\mathscr{G} \times_{\mathscr{E}}\mathscr{E}') \]

may be regarded as a functor in \(\mathscr{E}',\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G}\), from the category \(\mathbf{Cat}_{/\mathscr{E}}^\circ\times\mathbf{Cat}_{/\mathscr{E}}^\circ\times\mathbf{Cat}_{/\mathscr{E}}\) into the category \(\mathbf{Cat}\), isomorphic to the functor defined by the expression \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{F}\times_{\mathscr{E}}\mathscr{E}',\mathscr{G})\). In particular, for \(\mathscr{F}\), \(\mathscr{G}\) fixed, one obtains a functor in \(\mathscr{E}'\), \(\mathscr{E}'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}''/-}(\mathscr{F}',\mathscr{G}')= \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}'/-}(\mathscr{F}\times_{\mathscr{E}} \mathscr{E}',\mathscr{G}\times_{\mathscr{E}}\mathscr{E}')\), and in particular the \(\mathscr{E}\)-functor of projection \(\lambda\colon \mathscr{E}'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{E}\) defines a morphism, i.e. a functor

\[ \lambda^*_{\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G}}\colon \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}'/-}(\mathscr{F}',\mathscr{G}') \]
which we are going to make explicit. For the sets of objects of the two sides, it is the map
\[ f\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} f'=f\times_{\mathscr{E}}\mathscr{E}' \]

which expresses the functorial dependence of \(\mathscr{F}\times_{\mathscr{E}}\mathscr{E}'\) on the object \(\mathscr{F}\) over \(\mathscr{E}\). On the other hand, let us consider two \(\mathscr{E}\)-functors
\[ f,g\colon\mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{G} \]
and a homomorphism of \(\mathscr{E}\)-functors
\[ u\colon f\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} g, \]
we will make explicit the corresponding homomorphism of \(\mathscr{E}'\)-functors:
\[ u'\colon f'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} g'. \]
For every
\[ \xi'=(\xi,S')\in\operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{F}') \]
with
\[ \xi\in\operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{F}),\quad S'\in\operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{E}'),\quad p(\xi)=\lambda(S')=S \]
the morphism
\[ u'(\xi')\colon f'(\xi')=(f(\xi),S')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} g'(\xi')=(g(\xi),S')\quad \text{in } \mathscr{G}' \]
is defined by the formula
\[ u'(\xi')=(u(\xi),\mathrm{id}_{S'}) \]
(which is indeed an \(S'\)-morphism in \(\mathscr{G}'\), since \(q(u(\xi))=\lambda(\mathrm{id}_{S'})=\mathrm{id}_S\)).
Let us now consider an arbitrary \(\mathscr{E}\)-functor
\[ \lambda'\colon\mathscr{E}''\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{E}' \]
and the corresponding functor

\[ \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}'/-}(\mathscr{F}\times_{\mathscr{E}}\mathscr{E}', \mathscr{G}\times_{\mathscr{E}}\mathscr{E}')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}''/-}(\mathscr{F}\times_{\mathscr{E}}\mathscr{E}'', \mathscr{G}\times_{\mathscr{E}}\mathscr{E}''), \]

I claim that this functor is none other than the functor one obtains by the preceding procedure, starting from \(\mathscr{F}'\) and \(\mathscr{G}'\) over \(\mathscr{E}'\) and regarding \(\mathscr{E}''\) as a category over \(\mathscr{E}'\), taking into account the isomorphisms of « transitivity of base change »:

\[ \mathscr{F}'\times_{\mathscr{E}'}\mathscr{E}''\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \mathscr{F}''=\mathscr{F}\times_{\mathscr{E}}\mathscr{E}''\quad \text{and}\quad\mathscr{G}'\times_{\mathscr{E}'}\mathscr{E}''\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow}\mathscr{G}'' =\mathscr{G}\times_{\mathscr{E}}\mathscr{E}'', \]
which imply a canonical isomorphism

\[ \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}''/-}(\mathscr{F}'\times_{\mathscr{E}'}\mathscr{E}'', \mathscr{G}'\times_{\mathscr{E}'}\mathscr{E}'')\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}''/-}(\mathscr{F}\times_{\mathscr{E}}\mathscr{E}'', \mathscr{G}\times_{\mathscr{E}}\mathscr{E}'') \]
The verification of this compatibility is immediate, and left to the reader.

The functors we have just defined are compatible with the pairings defined in the preceding article; more precisely, if \(\mathscr{F}\), \(\mathscr{G}\), \(\mathscr{H}\) are categories over \(\mathscr{E}\) and if we set
\[ \mathscr{F}'=\mathscr{F}\times_{\mathscr{E}}\mathscr{E}',\quad \mathscr{G}'= \mathscr{G}\times_{\mathscr{E}}\mathscr{E}',\quad \mathscr{H}'= \mathscr{H}\times_{\mathscr{E}}\mathscr{E}', \]
we have commutativity in the following diagram of functors:

\[ \xymatrix{{\mathbf{Hom}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G})\times \mathbf{Hom}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{G},\mathscr{H})}\ar[r]\ar[d]_{\lambda^*_{\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G}}\times \lambda^*_{\mathscr{G},\mathscr{H}}}&{\mathbf{Hom}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{H})}\ar[d]^{\lambda^*_{\mathscr{F},\mathscr{H}}}\\{\mathbf{Hom}_{\mathscr{E}'/-}(\mathscr{F}',\mathscr{G}')\times \mathbf{Hom}_{\mathscr{E}'/-}(\mathscr{G}',\mathscr{H}')}\ar[r]&{\mathbf{Hom}_{\mathscr{E}'/-}(\mathscr{F}',\mathscr{H}')}} \]
where the horizontal arrows are the composition-functors defined in the preceding article. This commutativity is expressed by the formulas
\[ (gf)'=g'f' \]

for \(f\in\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{E}}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G})\), \(g\in\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{E}}(\mathscr{G},\mathscr{H})\), (a formula which simply expresses the functoriality of base change), and the formula
\[ (v*u)'=v'*u' \]

when \(u\colon f\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} f_1\) is an arrow of \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G})\) and \(v\colon g\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} g_1\) an arrow of \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{G},\mathscr{H})\). The verification of this formula follows easily from the definitions.

In what follows, we shall be interested above all in \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G})\) (and certain noteworthy subcategories of it) when \(\mathscr{F}=\mathscr{E}\), and for this reason we introduce a special notation:

\[ \mathbf{\Gamma}(\mathscr{G}/\mathscr{E})= \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}}(\mathscr{E},\mathscr{G}),\quad \Gamma(\mathscr{G}/\mathscr{E})=\operatorname{Ob}(\mathbf{\Gamma}(\mathscr{G}/\mathscr{E}))= \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{E}}(\mathscr{E},\mathscr{G}). \]

blocks.title: Remarks
blocks.html:
Remarks 3

When \(\mathscr{E}\) is a punctual category, i.e. \(\operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{E})\) and \(\operatorname{Fl}(\mathscr{E})\) reduced to a single element, which also means that \(\mathscr{E}\) is a final object of the category \(\mathbf{Cat}\), then the datum of a category over \(\mathscr{E}\) is equivalent to the datum of a category tout court, (since there will be a unique functor from \(\mathscr{F}\) to \(\mathscr{E}\)). More precisely, \(\mathbf{Cat}_{/\mathscr{E}}\) is then isomorphic to \(\mathbf{Cat}\). Moreover, the categories \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G})\) are then none other than the \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G})\). Let us then recall that the fundamental formula

\[ \operatorname{Hom}(\mathscr{H},\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G}))\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}(\mathscr{F}\times\mathscr{H},\mathscr{G}) \]

(an isomorphism functorial in the three arguments appearing in it), allows one to interpret \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G})\) axiomatically, in terms internal to the category \(\mathbf{Cat}\), so that the known formalism of the categories \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}\) appears as a particular case of a formalism valid in categories such as \(\mathbf{Cat}\), where « \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}\) objects » (defined by the preceding formula) exist. There is an analogous interpretation of \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G})\) when one again supposes \(\mathscr{E}\) arbitrary, by the formula

\[ \operatorname{Hom}(\mathscr{H},\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G}))\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{E}}(\mathscr{F}\times\mathscr{H},\mathscr{G}) \]

(an isomorphism functorial in the three arguments). In this way, the formal properties set forth in 2, 3 are particular cases of more general results, valid in categories where the objects \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G})\) (when \(\mathscr{F}\), \(\mathscr{G}\) are two objects of the category above a third \(\mathscr{E}\)) exist.

pages.title:
Fibre-categories; equivalence of
\(\mathscr{E}\)-categories
pages.html:
4 Fibre-categories; equivalence of \(\mathscr{E}\)-categories

Let \(\mathscr{F}\) be a category over \(\mathscr{E}\), and let \(S \in \operatorname{Ob}({\mathscr{E}})\). One calls fibre-category of \(\mathscr{F}\) at \(S\) the subcategory \(\mathscr{F}_S\) of \(\mathscr{F}\) inverse image of the punctual subcategory of \(\mathscr{E}\) defined by \(S\). Thus the objects of \(\mathscr{F}_S\) are the objects \(\xi\) of \(\mathscr{F}\) such that \(p(\xi)=S\), its morphisms are the morphisms \(u\) of \(\mathscr{F}\) such that \(p(u)=\mathrm{id}_S\), i.e. the \(S\)-morphisms in \(\mathscr{F}\). Of course, \(\mathscr{F}_S\) is canonically isomorphic to the fibred product \(\mathscr{F} \times_{\mathscr{E}} \{S\}\), where \(\{S\}\) denotes the punctual subcategory of \(\mathscr{E}\) defined by \(S\), equipped with its inclusion functor into \(\mathscr{E}\). It follows (taking into account the transitivity of base change) that if one makes the base change \(\lambda\colon \mathscr{E}' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{E}\), then for every \(S' \in \operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{E}')\), the projection \(\mathrm{pr}_1\colon \mathscr{F}' = \mathscr{F} \times_{\mathscr{E}} \mathscr{E}' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{F}\) induces an isomorphism

\[ \mathscr{F}'_{S'} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{F}_S \qquad (\text{where $S=\lambda(S')$}). \]
Proposition 1

Let \(f\colon \mathscr{F} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{G}\) be an \(\mathscr{E}\)-functor. If \(f\) is fully faithful, then for every base change \(\mathscr{E}' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{E}\), the corresponding functor \(f' \colon \mathscr{F}' = \mathscr{F} \times_{\mathscr{E}} \mathscr{E}' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{G}' = \mathscr{G} \times_{\mathscr{E}} \mathscr{E}'\) is fully faithful.

The verification is immediate; more generally, one can show that any inverse limit of fully faithful functors (here, \(f\) and the identity functors in \(\mathscr{E},\mathscr{E}'\)) is a fully faithful functor.

One will note that the assertion analogous to 4.1, where “ fully faithful ” is replaced by “ equivalence of categories ”, is false, already for \(\mathscr{G}=\mathscr{E}\). However:

Proposition 2

Let \(f \colon \mathscr{F} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{G}\) be an \(\mathscr{E}\)-functor. The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    There exists an \(\mathscr{E}\)-functor \(g \colon \mathscr{G} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{F}\) and \(\mathscr{E}\)-isomorphisms

    \[ gf \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \mathrm{id}_{\mathscr{F}}, \quad fg \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \mathrm{id}_{\mathscr{G}} . \]
  2. (ii)

    For every category \(\mathscr{E}'\) over \(\mathscr{E}\), the functor

    \[ f' = f \times_{\mathscr{E}} \mathscr{E}' \colon \mathscr{F}' = \mathscr{F} \times_{\mathscr{E}} \mathscr{E}' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{G}'=\mathscr{G} \times_{\mathscr{E}} \mathscr{E}' \]

    is an equivalence of categories.

  3. (iii)

    \(f\) is an equivalence of categories, and for every \(S \in \operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{E})\), the functor \(f_S \colon \mathscr{F}_S \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{G}_S\) induced by \(f\) is an equivalence of categories.

  4. (iii bis)

    \(f\) is fully faithful, and for every \(S \in \operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{E})\) and every \(\eta\in\operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{G}_S)\), there exists a \(\xi \in \operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{F}_S)\) and an \(S\)-isomorphism \(u \colon f(\xi) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \eta\).

Proof

Evidently (i) implies that \(f\) is an equivalence of categories (a notion which is defined by the same condition, but where the isomorphisms of functors are not constrained to be \(\mathscr{E}\)-morphisms). On the other hand, it follows from the functorialities of the preceding number that condition (i) is preserved after base change \(\mathscr{E}'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{E}\). It follows that (i)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(ii). Evidently (ii)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(iii), for it suffices to take \(\mathscr{E}' = \mathscr{E}\) and \(\mathscr{E}'=\{S\}\). It is even more trivial that (iii)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(iii bis); it remains to prove that (iii bis)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(i). For this, let us choose for every \(\eta \in \operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{G})\) a \(g(\eta) \in \operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{F})\) and an isomorphism \(u(\eta)\colon f(g(\eta))\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\eta\) which is such that \(q(u(\eta)) = \mathrm{id}_S\), where \(S=q(\eta)\). This is possible thanks to the second condition (iii bis). As is well known and immediate, the fact that \(f\) is fully faithful implies that \(g\) can in a unique way be regarded as a functor from \(\mathscr{G}\) to \(\mathscr{F}\), in such a way that the \(u(\eta)\) define a functorial homomorphism (hence an isomorphism) \(u\colon fg \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \mathrm{id}_{\mathscr{G}}\). Moreover, by construction \(g\) is an \(\mathscr{E}\)-functor and \(u\) an \(\mathscr{E}\)-homomorphism. To the preceding data there then corresponds a functorial isomorphism \(v\colon gf \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{id}_{\mathscr{F}}\), defined by the condition that \(f*v=u*f\), and one verifies at once that it is likewise an \(\mathscr{E}\)-morphism, q.e.d.

Definition 3

If the preceding conditions are satisfied, one says that \(f\) is an equivalence of categories over \(\mathscr{E}\), or an \(\mathscr{E}\)-equivalence.

Corollary 4

Suppose that the projection functor \(p\colon \mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{E}\) is a transportable functor, i.e. that for every isomorphism \(\alpha\colon T \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) in \(\mathscr{E}\) and every object \(\xi\) in \(\mathscr{F}_T\), there exists an isomorphism \(u\) in \(\mathscr{F}\) with source \(\xi\) such that \(p(u)=\alpha\). Then every \(\mathscr{E}\)-functor \(f\colon \mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{G}\) which is an equivalence of categories is an \(\mathscr{E}\)-equivalence.

This follows from criterion (iii bis).

Corollary 5

Let \(f\colon \mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{G}\) be an \(\mathscr{E}\)-equivalence. Then for every category \(\mathscr{H}\) over \(\mathscr{E}\), the corresponding functors:

\begin{align*} \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{G},\mathscr{H}) & \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{H}) \\ \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{H},\mathscr{F}) & \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{H},\mathscr{G}) \end{align*}

(cf. 2) are equivalences of categories.

This follows from criterion (i) by the usual reasoning.

blocks.html: 4 Fibre-categories; equivalence of \(\mathscr{E}\)-categories

Let \(\mathscr{F}\) be a category over \(\mathscr{E}\), and let \(S \in \operatorname{Ob}({\mathscr{E}})\). One calls fibre-category of \(\mathscr{F}\) at \(S\) the subcategory \(\mathscr{F}_S\) of \(\mathscr{F}\) inverse image of the punctual subcategory of \(\mathscr{E}\) defined by \(S\). Thus the objects of \(\mathscr{F}_S\) are the objects \(\xi\) of \(\mathscr{F}\) such that \(p(\xi)=S\), its morphisms are the morphisms \(u\) of \(\mathscr{F}\) such that \(p(u)=\mathrm{id}_S\), i.e. the \(S\)-morphisms in \(\mathscr{F}\). Of course, \(\mathscr{F}_S\) is canonically isomorphic to the fibred product \(\mathscr{F} \times_{\mathscr{E}} \{S\}\), where \(\{S\}\) denotes the punctual subcategory of \(\mathscr{E}\) defined by \(S\), equipped with its inclusion functor into \(\mathscr{E}\). It follows (taking into account the transitivity of base change) that if one makes the base change \(\lambda\colon \mathscr{E}' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{E}\), then for every \(S' \in \operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{E}')\), the projection \(\mathrm{pr}_1\colon \mathscr{F}' = \mathscr{F} \times_{\mathscr{E}} \mathscr{E}' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{F}\) induces an isomorphism
\[ \mathscr{F}'_{S'} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{F}_S \qquad (\text{where $S=\lambda(S')$}). \]

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1

Let \(f\colon \mathscr{F} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{G}\) be an \(\mathscr{E}\)-functor. If \(f\) is fully faithful, then for every base change \(\mathscr{E}' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{E}\), the corresponding functor \(f' \colon \mathscr{F}' = \mathscr{F} \times_{\mathscr{E}} \mathscr{E}' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{G}' = \mathscr{G} \times_{\mathscr{E}} \mathscr{E}'\) is fully faithful.

The verification is immediate; more generally, one can show that any inverse limit of fully faithful functors (here, \(f\) and the identity functors in \(\mathscr{E},\mathscr{E}'\)) is a fully faithful functor.
One will note that the assertion analogous to 4.1, where “ fully faithful ” is replaced by “ equivalence of categories ”, is false, already for \(\mathscr{G}=\mathscr{E}\). However:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2

Let \(f \colon \mathscr{F} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{G}\) be an \(\mathscr{E}\)-functor. The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    There exists an \(\mathscr{E}\)-functor \(g \colon \mathscr{G} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{F}\) and \(\mathscr{E}\)-isomorphisms

    \[ gf \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \mathrm{id}_{\mathscr{F}}, \quad fg \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \mathrm{id}_{\mathscr{G}} . \]
  2. (ii)

    For every category \(\mathscr{E}'\) over \(\mathscr{E}\), the functor

    \[ f' = f \times_{\mathscr{E}} \mathscr{E}' \colon \mathscr{F}' = \mathscr{F} \times_{\mathscr{E}} \mathscr{E}' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{G}'=\mathscr{G} \times_{\mathscr{E}} \mathscr{E}' \]

    is an equivalence of categories.

  3. (iii)

    \(f\) is an equivalence of categories, and for every \(S \in \operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{E})\), the functor \(f_S \colon \mathscr{F}_S \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{G}_S\) induced by \(f\) is an equivalence of categories.

  4. (iii bis)

    \(f\) is fully faithful, and for every \(S \in \operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{E})\) and every \(\eta\in\operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{G}_S)\), there exists a \(\xi \in \operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{F}_S)\) and an \(S\)-isomorphism \(u \colon f(\xi) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \eta\).
Proof

Evidently (i) implies that \(f\) is an equivalence of categories (a notion which is defined by the same condition, but where the isomorphisms of functors are not constrained to be \(\mathscr{E}\)-morphisms). On the other hand, it follows from the functorialities of the preceding number that condition (i) is preserved after base change \(\mathscr{E}'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{E}\). It follows that (i)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(ii). Evidently (ii)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(iii), for it suffices to take \(\mathscr{E}' = \mathscr{E}\) and \(\mathscr{E}'=\{S\}\). It is even more trivial that (iii)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(iii bis); it remains to prove that (iii bis)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(i). For this, let us choose for every \(\eta \in \operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{G})\) a \(g(\eta) \in \operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{F})\) and an isomorphism \(u(\eta)\colon f(g(\eta))\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\eta\) which is such that \(q(u(\eta)) = \mathrm{id}_S\), where \(S=q(\eta)\). This is possible thanks to the second condition (iii bis). As is well known and immediate, the fact that \(f\) is fully faithful implies that \(g\) can in a unique way be regarded as a functor from \(\mathscr{G}\) to \(\mathscr{F}\), in such a way that the \(u(\eta)\) define a functorial homomorphism (hence an isomorphism) \(u\colon fg \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \mathrm{id}_{\mathscr{G}}\). Moreover, by construction \(g\) is an \(\mathscr{E}\)-functor and \(u\) an \(\mathscr{E}\)-homomorphism. To the preceding data there then corresponds a functorial isomorphism \(v\colon gf \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{id}_{\mathscr{F}}\), defined by the condition that \(f*v=u*f\), and one verifies at once that it is likewise an \(\mathscr{E}\)-morphism, q.e.d.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 3

If the preceding conditions are satisfied, one says that \(f\) is an equivalence of categories over \(\mathscr{E}\), or an \(\mathscr{E}\)-equivalence.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4

Suppose that the projection functor \(p\colon \mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{E}\) is a transportable functor, i.e. that for every isomorphism \(\alpha\colon T \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) in \(\mathscr{E}\) and every object \(\xi\) in \(\mathscr{F}_T\), there exists an isomorphism \(u\) in \(\mathscr{F}\) with source \(\xi\) such that \(p(u)=\alpha\). Then every \(\mathscr{E}\)-functor \(f\colon \mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{G}\) which is an equivalence of categories is an \(\mathscr{E}\)-equivalence.
This follows from criterion (iii bis).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5

Let \(f\colon \mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{G}\) be an \(\mathscr{E}\)-equivalence. Then for every category \(\mathscr{H}\) over \(\mathscr{E}\), the corresponding functors:

\begin{align*} \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{G},\mathscr{H}) & \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{H}) \\ \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{H},\mathscr{F}) & \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{H},\mathscr{G}) \end{align*}

(cf. 2) are equivalences of categories.
This follows from criterion (i) by the usual reasoning.

pages.title:
Cartesian morphisms, inverse images, cartesian
functors
pages.html:
5 Cartesian morphisms, inverse images, cartesian functors

Let \(\mathscr{F}\) be a category over \(\mathscr{E}\), with projection-functor \(p\). .5cm

Definition 1

Consider a morphism

\[ \alpha\colon \eta\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\xi \]

in \(\mathscr{F}\), and let

\[ S = p(\xi), \quad T = p(\eta), \quad f=p(\alpha). \]

One says that \(\alpha\) is a cartesian morphism if for every \(\eta' \in \operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{F}_T)\) and every \(f\)-morphism \(u\colon \eta'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\xi\), there exists a unique \(T\)-morphism \(\overline{u}\colon \eta'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\eta\) such that \(u=\alpha\circ\overline{u}\).

This therefore means that for every \(\eta'\in\operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{F}_T)\), the map \(v \mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \alpha\circ v\):

\[ \tag{i} \operatorname{Hom}_T(\eta',\eta) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_f(\eta',\xi) \]

is bijective. It also means that the pair \((\eta,\alpha)\) represents the functor in \(\eta'\) \(\mathscr{F}^{\circ}_T \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbf{Ens}\) on the right-hand side. If for a given morphism \(f\colon T \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) in \(\mathscr{E}\), and a given \(\xi \in \operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{F}_S)\), there exists such a pair \((\eta,\alpha)\), i.e. a cartesian morphism \(\alpha\) in \(\mathscr{F}\) with target \(\xi\), such that \(p(\alpha)=f\), then \(\eta\) is determined in \(\mathscr{F}_T\) up to unique isomorphism. One then says that the inverse image of \(\xi\) by \(f\) exists, and an object \(\eta\) of \(\mathscr{F}_T\) equipped with a cartesian \(f\)-morphism \(\alpha\colon \eta \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \xi\) is called an inverse image of \(\xi\) by \(f\). Often one supposes chosen such an inverse image whenever it exists (\(\mathscr{F}\) being fixed); one will then denote the inverse image by symbols such as \(f^*_{\mathscr{F}}(\xi)\), or simply \(f^*(\xi)\) or \(\xi \times_S T\) when these notations do not give rise to confusion; the canonical morphism \(\alpha\colon \eta \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \xi\) will then be denoted, in what follows, by \(\alpha_f(\xi)\). If for every \(\xi \in \operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{F}_S)\), the inverse image of \(\xi\) by \(f\) exists, one will also say that the inverse image functor by \(f\) in \(\mathscr{F}\) exists, and \(f^*(\xi)\) then becomes a covariant functor in \(\xi\), from \(\mathscr{F}_S\) to \(\mathscr{F}_T\). This comes from the fact that the right-hand side in (i) depends covariantly on \(\xi\), i.e. more precisely denotes a functor from \(\mathscr{F}^{\circ}_T \times \mathscr{F}_S\) to \(\mathbf{Ens}\). This functorial dependence for \(f^*(\xi)\) is made explicit as follows: consider cartesian \(f\)-morphisms

\[ \alpha\colon \eta \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \xi, \quad \alpha'\colon \eta' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \xi' \]

and an \(S\)-morphism \(\lambda\colon \xi \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \xi'\), then there exists a unique \(T\)-morphism \(\mu\colon \eta\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\eta'\) such that one has

\[ \alpha' \mu = \lambda \alpha \]

(as follows from the fact that \(\alpha'\) is cartesian).

Let us also note the following immediate fact: consider a commutative diagram

\[ \xymatrix@C=1.2cm{{\xi}\ar[d]_-{\lambda}&{\eta}\ar[l]_-{\alpha}\ar[d]^-{\mu}\\{\xi'}&{\eta'}\ar[l]_-{\alpha'}} \]

in \(\mathscr{F}\), where \(\alpha\) and \(\alpha'\) are \(f\)-morphisms, and \(\lambda\) an \(S\)-isomorphism, \(\mu\) a \(T\)-isomorphism. In order for \(\alpha\) to be cartesian, it is necessary and sufficient that \(\alpha'\) be so.

Definition 2

An \(\mathscr{E}\)-functor \(F\colon \mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{G}\) is called a cartesian functor if it transforms cartesian morphisms into cartesian morphisms. One denotes by \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_\mathrm{cart}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G})\) the full subcategory of \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G})\) formed by the cartesian functors.

For example, considering \(\mathscr{E}\) as a category over \(\mathscr{E}\) by means of the identity functor, every morphism of \(\mathscr{E}\) is cartesian, hence a cartesian functor from \(\mathscr{E}\) to \(\mathscr{F}\) is a section functor \(F\colon \mathscr{E}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{F}\) which transforms every morphism of \(\mathscr{E}\) into a cartesian morphism; such a functor is called a cartesian section of \(\mathscr{F}\) over \(\mathscr{E}\).

Proposition 3

(i) A functor \(F\colon \mathscr{F} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{G}\) which is an \(\mathscr{E}\)-equivalence is a cartesian functor. (ii) Let \(F,G\) be two isomorphic \(\mathscr{E}\)-functors \(\mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{G}\). If one is cartesian, so is the other. (iii) The composite of two cartesian functors \(\mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{G}\) and \(\mathscr{G}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{H}\) is a cartesian functor.

Assertion (iii) is trivial from the definition, (ii) follows from the remark preceding 2, (i) follows easily from the definition and from the criterion 2 (iii); more precisely, a morphism \(\alpha\) in \(\mathscr{F}\) is cartesian if and only if \(F(\alpha)\) is.

Corollary 4

Let \(F \colon \mathscr{F} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{G}\) be an \(\mathscr{E}\)-equivalence. Then for every category \(\mathscr{H}\) over \(\mathscr{E}\), the corresponding functors \(G \mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} G \circ F\) and \(G \mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} F \circ G\) induce equivalences of categories:

\begin{eqnarray*} \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_\mathrm{cart}(\mathscr{G},\mathscr{H})\xrightarrow{\approx} \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_\mathrm{cart}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{H}) \\ \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_\mathrm{cart}(\mathscr{H},\mathscr{F})\xrightarrow{\approx} \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_\mathrm{cart}(\mathscr{H}, \mathscr{G}) \end{eqnarray*}

This is deduced in the usual way from 2 criterion (i) and from 3 (i) (ii) (iii). One can make precise that the \(\mathscr{E}\)-functor \(G\colon \mathscr{G} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{H}\) is cartesian if and only if \(G \circ F\) is, and likewise an \(\mathscr{E}\)-functor \(G\colon \mathscr{H} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{F}\) is cartesian if and only if \(F \circ G\) is.

It follows from 4 (iii) that if one considers the subcategory \(\mathbf{Cat}^\mathrm{cart}_{/\mathscr{E}}\) of \(\mathbf{Cat}_{/\mathscr{E}}\) whose objects are the same as those of \(\mathbf{Cat}_{/\mathscr{E}}\), and whose morphisms are the cartesian functors, then one has, as in 2, pairings:

\[ \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_\mathrm{cart}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G})\times \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_\mathrm{cart}(\mathscr{G},\mathscr{H}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_\mathrm{cart}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{H}) \]

induced by those of 2, allowing one to consider \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_\mathrm{cart}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G})\) as a functor in \(\mathscr{F}\), \(\mathscr{G}\), from the category \(\left(\mathbf{Cat}^\mathrm{cart}_{/\mathscr{E}} \right)^\circ \times \mathbf{Cat}^\mathrm{cart}_{/\mathscr{E}}\) to \(\mathbf{Cat}\). We shall need this remark above all for the case where \(\mathscr{F} = \mathscr{G}\):

Definition 5

Let \(\mathscr{F}\) be a category over \(\mathscr{E}\). One denotes by

\[ \varprojlim\mathscr{F}/\mathscr{E} \]

the category of cartesian \(\mathscr{E}\)-functors \(\mathscr{E}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{F}\), i.e. of the cartesian sections of \(\mathscr{F}\) over \(\mathscr{E}\).

According to what we have just said, \(\varprojlim\mathscr{F}/\mathscr{E}\) is a functor in \(\mathscr{F}\), from the category \(\mathbf{Cat}^\mathrm{cart}_{/\mathscr{E}}\) into the category \(\mathbf{Cat}\).

We shall see below the relations between this operation \(\varprojlim\) and the notion of inverse limit of categories, as well as numerous examples.

Definition 2

An \(\mathscr{E}\)-functor \(F\colon \mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{G}\) is called a cartesian functor if it transforms cartesian morphisms into cartesian morphisms.
