pages.title:
Fibred categories and prefibred
categories. Products and base change therein
pages.html:
6 Fibred categories and prefibred categories. Products and base change therein

Definition 1

A category \(\mathscr{F}\) over \(\mathscr{E}\) is called a fibred category (and one then says that the functor \(\mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{E}\) is fibring) if it satisfies the two following axioms:

  • \(\mathrm{Fib}_\mathrm{I}\) For every morphism \(f\colon T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) in \(\mathscr{E}\), the inverse image functor by \(f\) in \(\mathscr{F}\) exists.

  • \(\mathrm{Fib}_\mathrm{II}\) The composite of two cartesian morphisms is cartesian.

A category \(\mathscr{F}\) over \(\mathscr{E}\) satisfying the condition \(\mathrm{Fib}_\mathrm{I}\) is called a prefibred category over \(\mathscr{E}\).

If \(\mathscr{F}\) is a fibred category (resp. prefibred) over \(\mathscr{E}\), a subcategory \(\mathscr{G}\) of \(\mathscr{F}\) is called a fibred subcategory (resp. a prefibred subcategory) if it is a fibred (resp. prefibred) category over \(\mathscr{E}\), and if moreover the inclusion functor is cartesian. If for instance \(\mathscr{G}\) is a full subcategory of \(\mathscr{F}\), one sees that this means that for every morphism \(f\colon T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) in \(\mathscr{E}\) and for every \(\xi \in \operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{G}_S)\), \(f^*_{\mathscr{F}}(\xi)\) is \(T\)-isomorphic to an object of \(\mathscr{G}_T\). Another interesting case is the following: \(\mathscr{F}\) being a fibred category over \(\mathscr{E}\), consider the subcategory \(\mathscr{G}\) of \(\mathscr{F}\) having the same objects, and whose morphisms are the cartesian morphisms of \(\mathscr{F}\); in particular the morphisms of \(\mathscr{G}_S\) are the isomorphisms of \(\mathscr{F}_S\). One sees at once that this is indeed a fibred subcategory of \(\mathscr{F}\), for in the bijection

\[ \operatorname{Hom}_T(\eta',\eta) \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_f(\eta',\xi) \]

relative to a cartesian \(f\)-morphism \(\alpha\) in \(\mathscr{F}\), to the \(T\)-isomorphisms of the first member there correspond the cartesian morphisms of the second. By definition, the cartesian sections \(\mathscr{E}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{F}\) then correspond bijectively to the arbitrary \(\mathscr{E}\)-functors \(\mathscr{E}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{G}\) (but one will note that the natural functor

\[ \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{E},\mathscr{G})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_\mathrm{cart}(\mathscr{E},\mathscr{F})= \varprojlim(\mathscr{F}/\mathscr{E}) \]

is faithful, but in general is not fully faithful, i.e. is not an isomorphism).

Remarks

Let \(\mathscr{F}\) be a category over \(\mathscr{E}\). The following conditions are equivalent: (i) All the morphisms of \(\mathscr{F}\) are cartesian (ii) \(\mathscr{F}\) is a fibred category over \(\mathscr{E}\), and the \(\mathscr{F}_S\) are groupoids (i.e. every morphism in \(\mathscr{F}_S\) is an isomorphism). One then says that \(\mathscr{F}\) is a category fibred in groupoids over \(\mathscr{E}\). These are the ones that one encounters above all in the “theory of moduli”. If \(\mathscr{E}\) is a groupoid, one shows that conditions (i) and (ii) are also equivalent to the following: (iii) \(\mathscr{F}\) is a groupoid, and the projection functor \(p\colon \mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{E}\) is transportable (cf. 4). For instance, if \(\mathscr{E}\) and \(\mathscr{F}\) are groupoids such that \(\operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{E})\) and \(\operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{F})\) are reduced to a point, so that \(\mathscr{E}\) and \(\mathscr{F}\) are defined, up to isomorphism, by groups \(E\) and \(F\), and the functor \(p\colon \mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{E}\) is defined by a group homomorphism \(p\colon F\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} E\), then \(\mathscr{F}\) is fibred over \(\mathscr{E}\) if and only if \(p\) is surjective, i.e. if \(p\) defines an extension of the group \(E\) by the group \(G = \operatorname{Ker} p\).

Proposition 2

Let \(F \colon \mathscr{F} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{G}\) be an \(\mathscr{E}\)-equivalence. In order that \(\mathscr{F}\) be a fibred (resp. prefibred) category over \(\mathscr{E}\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(\mathscr{G}\) be so.

This follows easily from the definitions and from the remark pointed out above that a morphism \(\alpha\) in \(\mathscr{F}\) is cartesian if and only if \(F(\alpha)\) is.

Proposition 3

Let \(\mathscr{F}_1\), \(\mathscr{F}_2\) be two categories over \(\mathscr{E}\), and let \(\alpha = (\alpha_1,\alpha_2)\) be a morphism in \(\mathscr{F}=\mathscr{F}_1 \times_{\mathscr{E}} \mathscr{F}_2\). In order that \(\alpha\) be cartesian, it is necessary and sufficient that its components be so.

Indeed, let \(\xi_i\) be the target and \(\eta_i\) the source of \(\alpha_i\), and let \(f\colon T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be the morphism of \(\mathscr{E}\) such that \(\alpha_1\) and \(\alpha_2\) are \(f\)-morphisms. For every \(\eta'=(\eta'_1,\eta'_2)\) in \(\mathscr{F}_T\), one has a commutative diagram

\[ \xymatrix{{\mathrm{Hom}_T(\eta',\eta)}\ar[r]\ar[d]&{\mathrm{Hom}_f(\eta',\xi)}\ar[d]\\{\mathrm{Hom}_T(\eta'_1,\eta_1)\times\mathrm{Hom}_T(\eta'_2,\eta_2)}\ar[r]&{\mathrm{Hom}_f(\eta'_1,\xi_1)\times \mathrm{Hom}_f(\eta'_2,\xi_2)}} \]

where the vertical arrows are bijections. Hence if one of the horizontal arrows is a bijection, so is the other. This already shows that if \(\alpha_1\), \(\alpha_2\) are cartesian (so that the second horizontal arrow is bijective) then \(\alpha\) is so. The converse is seen by taking in the above diagram \(\eta'_i=\eta_i\), whence \(\operatorname{Hom}_T(\eta'_i,\eta_i) \neq \emptyset\), first for \(i=2\) which proves that \(\alpha_1\) is cartesian, then for \(i=1\) which proves that \(\alpha_2\) is cartesian.

Corollary 4

Let \(\mathscr{F} = \mathscr{F}_1\times_{\mathscr{E}}\mathscr{F}_2\), and let \(F=(F_1,F_2)\) be an \(\mathscr{E}\)-functor \(\mathscr{G}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{F}\). In order that \(F\) be cartesian, it is necessary and sufficient that \(F_1\) and \(F_2\) be so. One thus obtains an isomorphism of categories

\[ \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_\mathrm{cart}(\mathscr{G}, \mathscr{F}_1 \times_{\mathscr{E}} \mathscr{F}_2) \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_\mathrm{cart}(\mathscr{G},\mathscr{F}_1) \times\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_\mathrm{cart}(\mathscr{G},\mathscr{F}_2) \]

and in particular (taking \(\mathscr{G}=\mathscr{E}\)) an isomorphism of categories

\[ \varprojlim(\mathscr{F}_1 \times_{\mathscr{E}} \mathscr{F}_2/\mathscr{E}) \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \varprojlim(\mathscr{F}_1/\mathscr{E}) \times \varprojlim(\mathscr{F}_2/\mathscr{E}) \]
Corollary 5

Let \(\mathscr{F}_1\) and \(\mathscr{F}_2\) be two fibred categories (resp. prefibred) above \(\mathscr{E}\), then their fibre product \(\mathscr{F}=\mathscr{F}_1 \times_{\mathscr{E}}\mathscr{F}_2\) is a fibred (resp. prefibred) category over \(\mathscr{E}\).

These results extend, moreover, to the case of the fibre product of an arbitrary family of categories over \(\mathscr{E}\).

Proposition 6

Let \(\mathscr{F}\) be a category over \(\mathscr{E}\), with projection functor \(p\), and let \(\lambda \colon \mathscr{E}' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{E}\) be a functor, consider \(\mathscr{F}'=\mathscr{F}\times_{\mathscr{E}}\mathscr{E}'\) as a category over \(\mathscr{E}'\) by means of the projection functor \(p'=p \times_{\mathscr{E}} \mathrm{id}_{\mathscr{E}'}\). Let \(\alpha'\) be a morphism of \(\mathscr{F}'\); in order that \(\alpha'\) be a cartesian morphism, it is necessary and sufficient that its image \(\alpha\) in \(\mathscr{F}\) be so.

The proof is immediate and left to the reader.

Corollary 7

For every cartesian functor \(F\colon \mathscr{F} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{G}\) of categories over \(\mathscr{E}\), the functor \(F'=F\times_{\mathscr{E}}\mathscr{E}'\) from \(\mathscr{F}'=\mathscr{F}\times_{\mathscr{E}}\mathscr{E}'\) into \(\mathscr{G}'=\mathscr{G}\times_{\mathscr{E}}\mathscr{E}'\) is cartesian.

Consequently, the functor \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}'}(\mathscr{F}',\mathscr{G}')\) considered in the 3 induces a functor

\[ \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_\mathrm{cart}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_\mathrm{cart}(\mathscr{F}',\mathscr{G}') \mathrm{;} \]

in other words, for \(\mathscr{F}\), \(\mathscr{G}\) fixed, one may consider

\[ \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_\mathrm{cart}(\mathscr{F} \times_{\mathscr{E}} \mathscr{E}', \mathscr{G}\times_{\mathscr{E}} \mathscr{E}') \]

as a functor in \(\mathscr{E}'\), from the category \({\mathbf{Cat}_{/\mathscr{E}}}^\circ\) into \(\mathbf{Cat}\). If one also lets \(\mathscr{F}\), \(\mathscr{G}\) vary, one finds a functor from the category \({\mathbf{Cat}_{/\mathscr{E}}}^\circ \times \big( \mathbf{Cat}^\mathrm{cart}_{/\mathscr{E}} \big)^\circ \times \mathbf{Cat}^\mathrm{cart}_{/\mathscr{E}}\) into \(\mathbf{Cat}\). When one takes into account the isomorphism

\[ \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{E}'}(\mathscr{F}',\mathscr{G}') \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_\mathscr{E}(\mathscr{F}\times_\mathscr{E}\mathscr{E}',\mathscr{G}) \]

envisaged in 3, then the cartesian \(\mathscr{E}'\)-functors from \(\mathscr{F}'\) into \(\mathscr{G}'\) correspond to the \(\mathscr{E}\)-functors \(\mathscr{F} \times_\mathscr{E} \mathscr{E}' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{G}\) that transform every morphism whose first projection is a cartesian morphism of \(\mathscr{F}\) into a cartesian morphism of \(\mathscr{G}\). Taking \(\mathscr{F}=\mathscr{E}\), one finds (after a change of notation):

Corollary 8

\(\varprojlim(\mathscr{F}'/\mathscr{E}')\) is isomorphic to the full subcategory of \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{E}',\mathscr{F})\) formed by the \(\mathscr{E}\)-functors \(\mathscr{E}'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{F}\) that transform arbitrary morphisms into cartesian morphisms. In particular, if \(\mathscr{F}\) is a fibred category and if \(\widetilde{\mathscr{F}}\) is the subcategory of \(\mathscr{F}\) whose morphisms are the cartesian morphisms of \(\mathscr{F}\), then one has a bijection

\[ \operatorname{Ob} \varprojlim(\mathscr{F}'/\mathscr{E}') \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{E}',\widetilde{\mathscr{F}}) \mathrm{.} \]

This makes precise the way in which the expression \(\varprojlim(\mathscr{F} \times_{\mathscr{E}} \mathscr{E}' / \mathscr{E}')\) must be considered as a functor in \(\mathscr{E}'\) and in \(\mathscr{F}\), from the category \({\mathbf{Cat}_{/\mathscr{E}}}^\circ \times \mathbf{Cat}^\mathrm{cart}_{/\mathscr{E}}\) into the category \(\mathbf{Cat}\). We shall see later a more complete functorial dependence with respect to \(\mathscr{E}'\), when \(\mathscr{F}\) is required to be a fibred category.

Corollary 9

Let \(\mathscr{F}\) be a fibred (resp. prefibred) category over \(\mathscr{E}\), then \(\mathscr{F}'=\mathscr{F}\times_{\mathscr{E}}\mathscr{E}'\) is a fibred (resp. prefibred) category over \(\mathscr{E}'\).

Proposition 10

Let \(\mathscr{F}\), \(\mathscr{G}\) be prefibred categories over \(\mathscr{E}\), \(F\) a cartesian \(\mathscr{E}\)-functor from \(\mathscr{F}\) into \(\mathscr{G}\). In order that \(F\) be faithful, (resp. fully faithful, resp. an \(\mathscr{E}\)-equivalence) it is necessary and sufficient that for every \(S\in \operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{E})\), the induced functor \(F_S\colon \mathscr{F}_S\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{G}_S\) be faithful (resp. fully faithful, resp. an equivalence).

Immediate proof from the definitions.

To conclude this article, we give some properties of fibred categories, using the axiom \(\mathrm{Fib}_{\mathrm{II}}\).

Proposition 11

Let \(\mathscr{F}\) be a prefibred category over \(\mathscr{E}\). In order that \(\mathscr{F}\) be fibred, it is necessary and sufficient that it satisfy the following condition:

\(\mathrm{Fib}_{\mathrm{II}}'\): Let \(\alpha\colon\eta\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\xi\) be a cartesian morphism in \(\mathscr{F}\) above the morphism \(f\colon T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) of \(\mathscr{E}\). For every morphism \(g\colon U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T\) in \(\mathscr{E}\), and every \(\zeta\in\operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{F}_U)\), the map \(u\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}\alpha\circ u\):

\[ \operatorname{Hom}_g(\zeta,\eta)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Hom}_{fg}(\zeta,\xi) \]

is bijective.

In other words, in a fibred category over \(\mathscr{E}\), the cartesian diagrams are characterized by a property that is a priori stronger than that of the definition (which one obtains by taking \(g=\mathrm{id}_T\) in the statement above).

Corollary 12

Let \(\mathscr{F}\) be a category over \(\mathscr{E}\), \(\alpha\) a morphism in \(\mathscr{F}\). In order that \(\alpha\) be an isomorphism, it is necessary that \(p(\alpha)=f\) be an isomorphism and that \(\alpha\) be cartesian; the converse is true if \(\mathscr{F}\) is fibred over \(\mathscr{E}\).

Indeed, if \(\alpha\) is an isomorphism, so evidently is \(f=p(\alpha)\); for every \(\eta'\in\operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{F}_T)\), the map \(u\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \alpha\circ u\)

\[ \operatorname{Hom}(\eta',\eta)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}(\eta',\xi) \]

is bijective; since \(f\) is an isomorphism, one sees at once that an element of the first member is a \(T\)-morphism if and only if its image in the second is an \(f\)-morphism, so one thereby obtains a bijection

\[ \operatorname{Hom}_T(\eta',\eta)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Hom}_f(\eta',\xi) \]

which proves the first assertion. Conversely, suppose that \(f\) is an isomorphism and that \(\alpha\) satisfies the condition stated in \(\mathrm{Fib}_{\mathrm{II}'}\) (which therefore means, when \(\mathscr{F}\) is fibred over \(\mathscr{E}\), that \(\alpha\) is cartesian), then one sees at once that for every \(\zeta\in\operatorname{Ob}\mathscr{F}\), the map \(u\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \alpha\circ u\) from \(\operatorname{Hom}(\zeta,\eta)\) into \(\operatorname{Hom}(\zeta,\xi)\) is bijective, hence \(\alpha\) is an isomorphism.

Corollary 13

Let \(\alpha\colon\eta\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\xi\) and \(\beta\colon\zeta\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \eta\) be two composable morphisms in the fibred category \(\mathscr{F}\) over \(\mathscr{E}\). If \(\alpha\) is cartesian, then \(\beta\) is so if and only if \(\alpha\beta\) is so.

One uses the definition of cartesian morphisms in the strengthened form of 11.

blocks.html: 6 Fibred categories and prefibred categories. Products and base change therein

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1

A category \(\mathscr{F}\) over \(\mathscr{E}\) is called a fibred category (and one then says that the functor \(\mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{E}\) is fibring) if it satisfies the two following axioms:

  • \(\mathrm{Fib}_\mathrm{I}\) For every morphism \(f\colon T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) in \(\mathscr{E}\), the inverse image functor by \(f\) in \(\mathscr{F}\) exists.

  • \(\mathrm{Fib}_\mathrm{II}\) The composite of two cartesian morphisms is cartesian.

A category \(\mathscr{F}\) over \(\mathscr{E}\) satisfying the condition \(\mathrm{Fib}_\mathrm{I}\) is called a prefibred category over \(\mathscr{E}\).

If \(\mathscr{F}\) is a fibred category (resp. prefibred) over \(\mathscr{E}\), a subcategory \(\mathscr{G}\) of \(\mathscr{F}\) is called a fibred subcategory (resp. a prefibred subcategory) if it is a fibred (resp. prefibred) category over \(\mathscr{E}\), and if moreover the inclusion functor is cartesian. If for instance \(\mathscr{G}\) is a full subcategory of \(\mathscr{F}\), one sees that this means that for every morphism \(f\colon T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) in \(\mathscr{E}\) and for every \(\xi \in \operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{G}_S)\), \(f^*_{\mathscr{F}}(\xi)\) is \(T\)-isomorphic to an object of \(\mathscr{G}_T\). Another interesting case is the following: \(\mathscr{F}\) being a fibred category over \(\mathscr{E}\), consider the subcategory \(\mathscr{G}\) of \(\mathscr{F}\) having the same objects, and whose morphisms are the cartesian morphisms of \(\mathscr{F}\); in particular the morphisms of \(\mathscr{G}_S\) are the isomorphisms of \(\mathscr{F}_S\). One sees at once that this is indeed a fibred subcategory of \(\mathscr{F}\), for in the bijection
\[ \operatorname{Hom}_T(\eta',\eta) \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_f(\eta',\xi) \]

relative to a cartesian \(f\)-morphism \(\alpha\) in \(\mathscr{F}\), to the \(T\)-isomorphisms of the first member there correspond the cartesian morphisms of the second. By definition, the cartesian sections \(\mathscr{E}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{F}\) then correspond bijectively to the arbitrary \(\mathscr{E}\)-functors \(\mathscr{E}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{G}\) (but one will note that the natural functor

\[ \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{E},\mathscr{G})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_\mathrm{cart}(\mathscr{E},\mathscr{F})= \varprojlim(\mathscr{F}/\mathscr{E}) \]
is faithful, but in general is not fully faithful, i.e. is not an isomorphism).

blocks.title: Remarks
blocks.html:
Remarks

Let \(\mathscr{F}\) be a category over \(\mathscr{E}\). The following conditions are equivalent: (i) All the morphisms of \(\mathscr{F}\) are cartesian (ii) \(\mathscr{F}\) is a fibred category over \(\mathscr{E}\), and the \(\mathscr{F}_S\) are groupoids (i.e. every morphism in \(\mathscr{F}_S\) is an isomorphism). One then says that \(\mathscr{F}\) is a category fibred in groupoids over \(\mathscr{E}\). These are the ones that one encounters above all in the “theory of moduli”. If \(\mathscr{E}\) is a groupoid, one shows that conditions (i) and (ii) are also equivalent to the following: (iii) \(\mathscr{F}\) is a groupoid, and the projection functor \(p\colon \mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{E}\) is transportable (cf. 4). For instance, if \(\mathscr{E}\) and \(\mathscr{F}\) are groupoids such that \(\operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{E})\) and \(\operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{F})\) are reduced to a point, so that \(\mathscr{E}\) and \(\mathscr{F}\) are defined, up to isomorphism, by groups \(E\) and \(F\), and the functor \(p\colon \mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{E}\) is defined by a group homomorphism \(p\colon F\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} E\), then \(\mathscr{F}\) is fibred over \(\mathscr{E}\) if and only if \(p\) is surjective, i.e. if \(p\) defines an extension of the group \(E\) by the group \(G = \operatorname{Ker} p\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2

Let \(F \colon \mathscr{F} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{G}\) be an \(\mathscr{E}\)-equivalence. In order that \(\mathscr{F}\) be a fibred (resp. prefibred) category over \(\mathscr{E}\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(\mathscr{G}\) be so.
This follows easily from the definitions and from the remark pointed out above that a morphism \(\alpha\) in \(\mathscr{F}\) is cartesian if and only if \(F(\alpha)\) is.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3

Let \(\mathscr{F}_1\), \(\mathscr{F}_2\) be two categories over \(\mathscr{E}\), and let \(\alpha = (\alpha_1,\alpha_2)\) be a morphism in \(\mathscr{F}=\mathscr{F}_1 \times_{\mathscr{E}} \mathscr{F}_2\). In order that \(\alpha\) be cartesian, it is necessary and sufficient that its components be so.

Indeed, let \(\xi_i\) be the target and \(\eta_i\) the source of \(\alpha_i\), and let \(f\colon T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be the morphism of \(\mathscr{E}\) such that \(\alpha_1\) and \(\alpha_2\) are \(f\)-morphisms. For every \(\eta'=(\eta'_1,\eta'_2)\) in \(\mathscr{F}_T\), one has a commutative diagram

\[ \xymatrix{{\mathrm{Hom}_T(\eta',\eta)}\ar[r]\ar[d]&{\mathrm{Hom}_f(\eta',\xi)}\ar[d]\\{\mathrm{Hom}_T(\eta'_1,\eta_1)\times\mathrm{Hom}_T(\eta'_2,\eta_2)}\ar[r]&{\mathrm{Hom}_f(\eta'_1,\xi_1)\times \mathrm{Hom}_f(\eta'_2,\xi_2)}} \]

where the vertical arrows are bijections. Hence if one of the horizontal arrows is a bijection, so is the other. This already shows that if \(\alpha_1\), \(\alpha_2\) are cartesian (so that the second horizontal arrow is bijective) then \(\alpha\) is so. The converse is seen by taking in the above diagram \(\eta'_i=\eta_i\), whence \(\operatorname{Hom}_T(\eta'_i,\eta_i) \neq \emptyset\), first for \(i=2\) which proves that \(\alpha_1\) is cartesian, then for \(i=1\) which proves that \(\alpha_2\) is cartesian.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4

Let \(\mathscr{F} = \mathscr{F}_1\times_{\mathscr{E}}\mathscr{F}_2\), and let \(F=(F_1,F_2)\) be an \(\mathscr{E}\)-functor \(\mathscr{G}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{F}\). In order that \(F\) be cartesian, it is necessary and sufficient that \(F_1\) and \(F_2\) be so. One thus obtains an isomorphism of categories

\[ \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_\mathrm{cart}(\mathscr{G}, \mathscr{F}_1 \times_{\mathscr{E}} \mathscr{F}_2) \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_\mathrm{cart}(\mathscr{G},\mathscr{F}_1) \times\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_\mathrm{cart}(\mathscr{G},\mathscr{F}_2) \]

and in particular (taking \(\mathscr{G}=\mathscr{E}\)) an isomorphism of categories

\[ \varprojlim(\mathscr{F}_1 \times_{\mathscr{E}} \mathscr{F}_2/\mathscr{E}) \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \varprojlim(\mathscr{F}_1/\mathscr{E}) \times \varprojlim(\mathscr{F}_2/\mathscr{E}) \]

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5

Let \(\mathscr{F}_1\) and \(\mathscr{F}_2\) be two fibred categories (resp. prefibred) above \(\mathscr{E}\), then their fibre product \(\mathscr{F}=\mathscr{F}_1 \times_{\mathscr{E}}\mathscr{F}_2\) is a fibred (resp. prefibred) category over \(\mathscr{E}\).
These results extend, moreover, to the case of the fibre product of an arbitrary family of categories over \(\mathscr{E}\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 6

Let \(\mathscr{F}\) be a category over \(\mathscr{E}\), with projection functor \(p\), and let \(\lambda \colon \mathscr{E}' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{E}\) be a functor, consider \(\mathscr{F}'=\mathscr{F}\times_{\mathscr{E}}\mathscr{E}'\) as a category over \(\mathscr{E}'\) by means of the projection functor \(p'=p \times_{\mathscr{E}} \mathrm{id}_{\mathscr{E}'}\). Let \(\alpha'\) be a morphism of \(\mathscr{F}'\); in order that \(\alpha'\) be a cartesian morphism, it is necessary and sufficient that its image \(\alpha\) in \(\mathscr{F}\) be so.
The proof is immediate and left to the reader.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 7

For every cartesian functor \(F\colon \mathscr{F} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{G}\) of categories over \(\mathscr{E}\), the functor \(F'=F\times_{\mathscr{E}}\mathscr{E}'\) from \(\mathscr{F}'=\mathscr{F}\times_{\mathscr{E}}\mathscr{E}'\) into \(\mathscr{G}'=\mathscr{G}\times_{\mathscr{E}}\mathscr{E}'\) is cartesian.

Consequently, the functor \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}'}(\mathscr{F}',\mathscr{G}')\) considered in the 3 induces a functor

\[ \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_\mathrm{cart}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_\mathrm{cart}(\mathscr{F}',\mathscr{G}') \mathrm{;} \]
in other words, for \(\mathscr{F}\), \(\mathscr{G}\) fixed, one may consider
\[ \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_\mathrm{cart}(\mathscr{F} \times_{\mathscr{E}} \mathscr{E}', \mathscr{G}\times_{\mathscr{E}} \mathscr{E}') \]

as a functor in \(\mathscr{E}'\), from the category \({\mathbf{Cat}_{/\mathscr{E}}}^\circ\) into \(\mathbf{Cat}\). If one also lets \(\mathscr{F}\), \(\mathscr{G}\) vary, one finds a functor from the category \({\mathbf{Cat}_{/\mathscr{E}}}^\circ \times \big( \mathbf{Cat}^\mathrm{cart}_{/\mathscr{E}} \big)^\circ \times \mathbf{Cat}^\mathrm{cart}_{/\mathscr{E}}\) into \(\mathbf{Cat}\). When one takes into account the isomorphism
\[ \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{E}'}(\mathscr{F}',\mathscr{G}') \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_\mathscr{E}(\mathscr{F}\times_\mathscr{E}\mathscr{E}',\mathscr{G}) \]

envisaged in 3, then the cartesian \(\mathscr{E}'\)-functors from \(\mathscr{F}'\) into \(\mathscr{G}'\) correspond to the \(\mathscr{E}\)-functors \(\mathscr{F} \times_\mathscr{E} \mathscr{E}' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{G}\) that transform every morphism whose first projection is a cartesian morphism of \(\mathscr{F}\) into a cartesian morphism of \(\mathscr{G}\). Taking \(\mathscr{F}=\mathscr{E}\), one finds (after a change of notation):

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 8

\(\varprojlim(\mathscr{F}'/\mathscr{E}')\) is isomorphic to the full subcategory of \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{E}',\mathscr{F})\) formed by the \(\mathscr{E}\)-functors \(\mathscr{E}'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{F}\) that transform arbitrary morphisms into cartesian morphisms. In particular, if \(\mathscr{F}\) is a fibred category and if \(\widetilde{\mathscr{F}}\) is the subcategory of \(\mathscr{F}\) whose morphisms are the cartesian morphisms of \(\mathscr{F}\), then one has a bijection

\[ \operatorname{Ob} \varprojlim(\mathscr{F}'/\mathscr{E}') \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{E}',\widetilde{\mathscr{F}}) \mathrm{.} \]

This makes precise the way in which the expression \(\varprojlim(\mathscr{F} \times_{\mathscr{E}} \mathscr{E}' / \mathscr{E}')\) must be considered as a functor in \(\mathscr{E}'\) and in \(\mathscr{F}\), from the category \({\mathbf{Cat}_{/\mathscr{E}}}^\circ \times \mathbf{Cat}^\mathrm{cart}_{/\mathscr{E}}\) into the category \(\mathbf{Cat}\). We shall see later a more complete functorial dependence with respect to \(\mathscr{E}'\), when \(\mathscr{F}\) is required to be a fibred category.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 9

Let \(\mathscr{F}\) be a fibred (resp. prefibred) category over \(\mathscr{E}\), then \(\mathscr{F}'=\mathscr{F}\times_{\mathscr{E}}\mathscr{E}'\) is a fibred (resp. prefibred) category over \(\mathscr{E}'\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 10

Let \(\mathscr{F}\), \(\mathscr{G}\) be prefibred categories over \(\mathscr{E}\), \(F\) a cartesian \(\mathscr{E}\)-functor from \(\mathscr{F}\) into \(\mathscr{G}\). In order that \(F\) be faithful, (resp. fully faithful, resp. an \(\mathscr{E}\)-equivalence) it is necessary and sufficient that for every \(S\in \operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{E})\), the induced functor \(F_S\colon \mathscr{F}_S\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{G}_S\) be faithful (resp. fully faithful, resp. an equivalence).
Immediate proof from the definitions.
To conclude this article, we give some properties of fibred categories, using the axiom \(\mathrm{Fib}_{\mathrm{II}}\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 11

Let \(\mathscr{F}\) be a prefibred category over \(\mathscr{E}\). In order that \(\mathscr{F}\) be fibred, it is necessary and sufficient that it satisfy the following condition:

\(\mathrm{Fib}_{\mathrm{II}}'\): Let \(\alpha\colon\eta\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\xi\) be a cartesian morphism in \(\mathscr{F}\) above the morphism \(f\colon T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) of \(\mathscr{E}\). For every morphism \(g\colon U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T\) in \(\mathscr{E}\), and every \(\zeta\in\operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{F}_U)\), the map \(u\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}\alpha\circ u\):

\[ \operatorname{Hom}_g(\zeta,\eta)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Hom}_{fg}(\zeta,\xi) \]

is bijective.

In other words, in a fibred category over \(\mathscr{E}\), the cartesian diagrams are characterized by a property that is a priori stronger than that of the definition (which one obtains by taking \(g=\mathrm{id}_T\) in the statement above).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 12

Let \(\mathscr{F}\) be a category over \(\mathscr{E}\), \(\alpha\) a morphism in \(\mathscr{F}\). In order that \(\alpha\) be an isomorphism, it is necessary that \(p(\alpha)=f\) be an isomorphism and that \(\alpha\) be cartesian; the converse is true if \(\mathscr{F}\) is fibred over \(\mathscr{E}\).

Indeed, if \(\alpha\) is an isomorphism, so evidently is \(f=p(\alpha)\); for every \(\eta'\in\operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{F}_T)\), the map \(u\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \alpha\circ u\)
\[ \operatorname{Hom}(\eta',\eta)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}(\eta',\xi) \]

is bijective; since \(f\) is an isomorphism, one sees at once that an element of the first member is a \(T\)-morphism if and only if its image in the second is an \(f\)-morphism, so one thereby obtains a bijection
\[ \operatorname{Hom}_T(\eta',\eta)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Hom}_f(\eta',\xi) \]

which proves the first assertion. Conversely, suppose that \(f\) is an isomorphism and that \(\alpha\) satisfies the condition stated in \(\mathrm{Fib}_{\mathrm{II}'}\) (which therefore means, when \(\mathscr{F}\) is fibred over \(\mathscr{E}\), that \(\alpha\) is cartesian), then one sees at once that for every \(\zeta\in\operatorname{Ob}\mathscr{F}\), the map \(u\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \alpha\circ u\) from \(\operatorname{Hom}(\zeta,\eta)\) into \(\operatorname{Hom}(\zeta,\xi)\) is bijective, hence \(\alpha\) is an isomorphism.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 13

Let \(\alpha\colon\eta\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\xi\) and \(\beta\colon\zeta\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \eta\) be two composable morphisms in the fibred category \(\mathscr{F}\) over \(\mathscr{E}\). If \(\alpha\) is cartesian, then \(\beta\) is so if and only if \(\alpha\beta\) is so.
One uses the definition of cartesian morphisms in the strengthened form of 11.

pages.title: Cleaved categories over \(\mathscr{E}\)
pages.html:
7 Cleaved categories over \(\mathscr{E}\)

Definition 1

Let \(\mathscr{F}\) be a category over \(\mathscr{E}\). One calls a cleavage of \(\mathscr{F}\) over \(\mathscr{E}\) a function that attaches to every \(f\in\operatorname{Fl}(\mathscr{E})\) an inverse image functor for \(f\) in \(\mathscr{F}\), say \(f^*\). The cleavage is said to be normalized if \(f=\mathrm{id}_S\) implies \(f^*=\mathrm{id}_{\mathscr{F}_S}\). One calls a cleaved category (resp. a normalized cleaved category) a category \(\mathscr{F}\) over \(\mathscr{E}\) equipped with a cleavage (resp. with a normalized cleavage).

It is evident that \(\mathscr{F}\) admits a cleavage if and only if \(\mathscr{F}\) is prefibred over \(\mathscr{E}\), and then \(\mathscr{F}\) admits a normalized cleavage. The set of cleavages on \(\mathscr{F}\) is in one-to-one correspondence with the set of subsets \(K\) of \(\operatorname{Fl}(\mathscr{F})\) satisfying the following conditions:

  1. a)

    The \(\alpha\in K\) are cartesian morphisms.

  2. b)

    For every morphism \(f\colon T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) in \(\mathscr{E}\) and every \(\xi\in\operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{F}_S)\), there exists a unique \(f\)-morphism in \(K\), with target \(\xi\).

For the cleavage defined by \(K\) to be normalized, it is necessary and sufficient that \(K\) satisfy moreover the condition

  1. c)

    The identity morphisms in \(\mathscr{F}\) belong to \(K\).

The morphisms that are elements of \(K\) may be called the “transport morphisms” for the cleavage under consideration.

The notion of isomorphism of cleaved categories over \(\mathscr{E}\) is clear. More generally, one can define the morphisms of cleaved \(\mathscr{E}\)-categories as the functors of \(\mathscr{E}\)-categories \(\mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{G}\) that carry transport morphisms into transport morphisms. (These are in particular cartesian functors). In this way the cleaved categories over \(\mathscr{E}\) are the objects of a category, the category of cleaved categories over \(\mathscr{E}\). The reader will make explicit the existence of products, tied to the fact that if a category over \(\mathscr{E}\) is a product of categories \(\mathscr{F}_i\) over \(\mathscr{E}\) each equipped with a cleavage, then \(\mathscr{F}\) is equipped with a corresponding natural cleavage. We also leave to the reader the task of making explicit the notion of base change in cleaved categories.

We shall denote by \(\alpha_f(\xi)\) the canonical morphism

\[ \alpha_f(\xi)\colon f^*(\xi)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\xi. \]

It is, as we have said, functorial in \(\xi\), i.e. one has a functorial homomorphism

\[ \alpha_f\colon i_Tf^*\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} i_S, \]

where for every \(S\in\operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{E})\), \(i_S\) denotes the inclusion functor

\[ i_S\colon\mathscr{F}_S\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{F} \]

Let us now consider morphisms

\[ f\colon T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\quad\text{ and }\quad g\colon U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T \]

in \(\mathscr{E}\), and let \(\xi\in\operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{F}_S)\), then there exists a unique \(U\)-morphism

\[ c_{f,g}(\xi)\colon g^*f^*(\xi)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} (fg)^*(\xi) \]

making the diagram commutative

\[ \xymatrix{{f^*(\xi)}\ar[d]_{\alpha_f(\xi)}& &{g^*(f^*(\xi))}\ar[ll]_{\alpha_g(f^*(\xi))}\ar[d]^{c_{f,g}(\xi)}\\{\xi}& &{(fg)^*(\xi)}\ar[ll]^{\alpha_{fg}(\xi)}} \]

(by virtue of the definition of \((fg)^*(\xi)\)). For variable \(\xi\), this homomorphism is functorial, i.e.: one has a homomorphism

\[ c_{f,g}\colon g^*f^*\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} (fg)^* \]

of functors \(\mathscr{F}_S\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{F}_U\). Let us note at once:

Proposition 2

For the cleaved category \(\mathscr{F}\) over \(\mathscr{E}\) to be fibred, it is necessary and sufficient that the \(c_{f,g}\) be isomorphisms.

From this one concludes, taking for \(f\) an isomorphism, for \(g\) its inverse, and considering the isomorphisms \(c_{f,g}\) and \(c_{g,f}\):

Corollary 3

If \(\mathscr{F}\) is a fibred cleaved category over \(\mathscr{E}\), then for every isomorphism \(f\colon T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) in \(\mathscr{E}\), \(f^*\) is an equivalence of categories \(\mathscr{F}_S\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{F}_T\).

Proposition 4

Let \(\mathscr{F}\) be a cleaved category over \(\mathscr{E}\). One has

\(\qquad\qquad A)\qquad \left\{\begin{array}{ccc}c_{f,\mathrm{id}_T}(\xi)&=&\alpha_{\mathrm{id}_T}(f^*(\xi))\\ c_{\mathrm{id}_S,f}(\xi)&=&f^*(\alpha_{\mathrm{id}_S}(\xi)) \end{array}\right.\)

\(\qquad\qquad B)\qquad c_{f,gh}(\xi)\cdot c_{g,h}(f^*(\xi))=c_{fg,h}(\xi)\cdot h^*(c_{f,g}(\xi))\)

(In these formulas, \(f,g,h\) denote morphisms

\[ V\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S \]

and \(\xi\) an object of \(\mathscr{F}_S\)).

The first and second relations, in the case of a normalized cleavage, take the simpler form

\[ \quad A')\qquad {c_{f,\mathrm{id}_T}=\mathrm{id}_{f^*},\quad c_{\mathrm{id}_S,f}=\mathrm{id}_{f^*}.\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad} \]

As for the third, it is visualized by the commutativity of the diagram

\[ \tag{D} \begin{split} \xymatrix@C=2.2cm{{h^*g^*f^*(\xi)}\ar[r]^-{c_{g,h}(f^*(\xi))}\ar[d]_{h^*(c_{f,g}(\xi))}&{(gh)^*(f^*(\xi))}\ar[d]^{c_{f,gh}(\xi)}\\{h^*(fg)^*(\xi)}\ar[r]_-{c_{fg,h}(\xi)}&{(fgh)^*(\xi)}} \end{split} \]

In the case of fibred categories (where the \(c_{f,g}\) are isomorphisms), this commutativity can be expressed intuitively by the fact that the successive use of the isomorphisms of the form \(c_{f,g}\) does not lead to “contradictory identifications”. One can also write this formula without the argument \(\xi\), by using the convolution product of homomorphisms of functors:

\[ c_{fg,h}\circ (h^**c_{f,g})=c_{f,gh}\circ(c_{g,h}*f^*). \]

.5cm The proof of the first two formulas 4 is trivial; let us sketch that of the third. For this, let us consider, in addition to the square \((D)\), the square of homomorphisms:

\[ \tag{D'} \begin{split} \xymatrix@C=2cm{{g^*f^*(\xi)}\ar[r]^-{\alpha_g(f^*(\xi))}\ar[d]_{c_{f,g}(\xi)}&{f^*(\xi)}\ar[d]^{\alpha_f(\xi)}\\{(fg)^*(\xi)}\ar[r]_-{\alpha_{fg}(\xi)}&{\xi}} \end{split} \]

which is commutative by definition of \(c_{f,g}(\xi)\). Let us consider the diagram obtained by joining the vertices of \((D)\) to the corresponding vertices of \((D')\) by the homomorphisms of the form \(\alpha\):

\[ \begin{array}{ll} \alpha_h(g^*f^*(\xi)),&\alpha_{gh}(f^*(\xi))\\ \alpha_h((fg)^*(\xi)),&\alpha_{fgh}(\xi). \end{array} \]

The four lateral faces of the cube thus obtained are also commutative: for the left-hand one, this comes from the fact that the left column of \((D)\) is deduced from the left column of \((D')\) by application of \(h\), and that \(\alpha_h\) is a functorial homomorphism; for the other three, it is nothing but the definition of the operations \(c\) of the three remaining sides of \((D)\). Thus the five faces of the cube other than the top face are commutative. It follows that the two \((fgh)\)-morphisms \(h^*g^*f^*(\xi)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}(fgh)^*(\xi)\) defined by \((D)\) have the same composite with \(\alpha_{fgh}(\xi)\colon (fgh)^*(\xi)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\xi\), hence they are equal by definition of \((fgh)^*\).

Let us restrict ourselves in what follows to normalized cleaved categories. Such a category gives rise to the following objects:

  1. a)

    A mapping \(S\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \mathscr{F}_S\) from \(\operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{E})\) into \(\mathbf{Cat}\).

  2. b)

    A mapping \(f\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} f^*\), associating to every \(f\in\operatorname{Fl}(\mathscr{E})\), with source \(T\) and target \(S\), a functor \(f^*\colon \mathscr{F}_S\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{F}_T\).

  3. c)

    A mapping \((f,g)\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} c_{f,g}\), associating to every pair of arrows \((f,g)\) of \(\mathscr{E}\), a functorial homomorphism \(c_{f,g}\colon g^*f^*\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} (fg)^*\).

Moreover these data satisfy the conditions expressed in formulas \(A')\) and \(B)\) given above. (N.B. If one had not restricted oneself to the case of a normalized cleavage, it would have been necessary to introduce an additional object, namely a function \(S\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \alpha_S\) which associates to every object \(S\) of \(\mathscr{E}\) a functorial homomorphism \(\alpha_S\colon (\mathrm{id}_S)^*\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{id}_{\mathscr{F}_S}\); condition \(A')\) would then be replaced by condition \(A)\)).

We shall now show how one can reconstitute (up to unique isomorphism) the normalized cloven category \(\mathscr{F}\) over \(\mathscr{E}\) by means of the preceding objects.

blocks.html: 7 Cleaved categories over \(\mathscr{E}\)

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1

Let \(\mathscr{F}\) be a category over \(\mathscr{E}\). One calls a cleavage of \(\mathscr{F}\) over \(\mathscr{E}\) a function that attaches to every \(f\in\operatorname{Fl}(\mathscr{E})\) an inverse image functor for \(f\) in \(\mathscr{F}\), say \(f^*\). The cleavage is said to be normalized if \(f=\mathrm{id}_S\) implies \(f^*=\mathrm{id}_{\mathscr{F}_S}\). One calls a cleaved category (resp. a normalized cleaved category) a category \(\mathscr{F}\) over \(\mathscr{E}\) equipped with a cleavage (resp. with a normalized cleavage).

It is evident that \(\mathscr{F}\) admits a cleavage if and only if \(\mathscr{F}\) is prefibred over \(\mathscr{E}\), and then \(\mathscr{F}\) admits a normalized cleavage. The set of cleavages on \(\mathscr{F}\) is in one-to-one correspondence with the set of subsets \(K\) of \(\operatorname{Fl}(\mathscr{F})\) satisfying the following conditions:

1. a)

    The \(\alpha\in K\) are cartesian morphisms.

  2. b)

    For every morphism \(f\colon T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) in \(\mathscr{E}\) and every \(\xi\in\operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{F}_S)\), there exists a unique \(f\)-morphism in \(K\), with target \(\xi\).
For the cleavage defined by \(K\) to be normalized, it is necessary and sufficient that \(K\) satisfy moreover the condition

1. c)

    The identity morphisms in \(\mathscr{F}\) belong to \(K\).
The morphisms that are elements of \(K\) may be called the “transport morphisms” for the cleavage under consideration.

The notion of isomorphism of cleaved categories over \(\mathscr{E}\) is clear. More generally, one can define the morphisms of cleaved \(\mathscr{E}\)-categories as the functors of \(\mathscr{E}\)-categories \(\mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{G}\) that carry transport morphisms into transport morphisms. (These are in particular cartesian functors). In this way the cleaved categories over \(\mathscr{E}\) are the objects of a category, the category of cleaved categories over \(\mathscr{E}\). The reader will make explicit the existence of products, tied to the fact that if a category over \(\mathscr{E}\) is a product of categories \(\mathscr{F}_i\) over \(\mathscr{E}\) each equipped with a cleavage, then \(\mathscr{F}\) is equipped with a corresponding natural cleavage. We also leave to the reader the task of making explicit the notion of base change in cleaved categories.
We shall denote by \(\alpha_f(\xi)\) the canonical morphism
\[ \alpha_f(\xi)\colon f^*(\xi)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\xi. \]
It is, as we have said, functorial in \(\xi\), i.e. one has a functorial homomorphism
\[ \alpha_f\colon i_Tf^*\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} i_S, \]
where for every \(S\in\operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{E})\), \(i_S\) denotes the inclusion functor
\[ i_S\colon\mathscr{F}_S\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{F} \]
Let us now consider morphisms
\[ f\colon T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\quad\text{ and }\quad g\colon U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T \]
in \(\mathscr{E}\), and let \(\xi\in\operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{F}_S)\), then there exists a unique \(U\)-morphism
\[ c_{f,g}(\xi)\colon g^*f^*(\xi)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} (fg)^*(\xi) \]
making the diagram commutative
\[ \xymatrix{{f^*(\xi)}\ar[d]_{\alpha_f(\xi)}& &{g^*(f^*(\xi))}\ar[ll]_{\alpha_g(f^*(\xi))}\ar[d]^{c_{f,g}(\xi)}\\{\xi}& &{(fg)^*(\xi)}\ar[ll]^{\alpha_{fg}(\xi)}} \]
(by virtue of the definition of \((fg)^*(\xi)\)). For variable \(\xi\), this homomorphism is functorial, i.e.: one has a homomorphism
\[ c_{f,g}\colon g^*f^*\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} (fg)^* \]
of functors \(\mathscr{F}_S\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{F}_U\). Let us note at once:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2

For the cleaved category \(\mathscr{F}\) over \(\mathscr{E}\) to be fibred, it is necessary and sufficient that the \(c_{f,g}\) be isomorphisms.
From this one concludes, taking for \(f\) an isomorphism, for \(g\) its inverse, and considering the isomorphisms \(c_{f,g}\) and \(c_{g,f}\):

blocks.html:
Corollary 3

If \(\mathscr{F}\) is a fibred cleaved category over \(\mathscr{E}\), then for every isomorphism \(f\colon T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) in \(\mathscr{E}\), \(f^*\) is an equivalence of categories \(\mathscr{F}_S\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{F}_T\).

  2. b)

    A mapping \(f\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} f^*\), associating to every \(f\in\operatorname{Fl}(\mathscr{E})\), with source \(T\) and target \(S\), a functor \(f^*\colon \mathscr{F}_S\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{F}_T\).

  3. c)

    A mapping \((f,g)\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} c_{f,g}\), associating to every pair of arrows \((f,g)\) of \(\mathscr{E}\), a functorial homomorphism \(c_{f,g}\colon g^*f^*\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} (fg)^*\).

pages.title:
Cleaved category defined by a pseudo-functor
\(\mathscr{E}^\circ\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathbf{Cat}\)
pages.html:
8 Cleaved category defined by a pseudo-functor \(\mathscr{E}^\circ\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathbf{Cat}\)

Let us call, for brevity, a pseudo-functor from \(\mathscr{E}^\circ\) into \(\mathbf{Cat}\) (one ought to say, normalized pseudo-functor), a set of data a), b), c) as above, satisfying the conditions \(A')\) and \(B)\). In the preceding section, we associated, to a normalized cleaved category over \(\mathscr{E}\), a pseudo-functor \(\mathscr{E}^\circ\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathbf{Cat}\); here we shall indicate the inverse construction. We shall leave to the reader the verification of most of the details, as well as of the fact that these constructions are indeed “inverse” to one another. To be precise, it would be appropriate to consider the pseudo-functors \(\mathscr{E}^\circ\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathbf{Cat}\) as the objects of a new category, and to show that our constructions yield equivalences, quasi-inverse to one another, between this latter and the category of cleaved categories over \(\mathscr{E}\), defined in the preceding section.

We set

\[ \mathscr{F}_\circ=\underset{S\in\operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{E})}\coprod \operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{F}(S)), \]

the sum set of the sets \(\operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{F}(S))\) (N.B. here we shall write \(\mathscr{F}(S)\) and not \(\mathscr{F}_S\) for the value at the object \(S\) of \(\mathscr{E}\) of the given pseudo-functor, in order to avoid notational confusions in what follows). We thus have an obvious map:

\[ p_\circ\colon\mathscr{F}_\circ\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Ob}\mathscr{E}. \]

Let

\[ \overline{\xi} =(S,\xi),\quad \overline{\eta}=(T,\eta)\qquad\qquad \text{(with $\xi\in\operatorname{Ob}\mathscr{F}(S)$, $\eta\in\operatorname{Ob}\mathscr{F}(T)$)} \]

be two elements of \(\mathscr{F}_\circ\), and let \(f\in\operatorname{Hom}(T,S)\); we shall set

\[ h_f(\overline{\eta},\overline{\xi})=\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{F}(T)}(\eta,f^*(\xi)). \]

If we have moreover a morphism \(g\colon U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T\) in \(\mathscr{E}\), and a \(\zeta\in\operatorname{Ob}\mathscr{F}(U)\), we define a map, denoted \((u,v)\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} u\circ v\):

\[ h_f(\overline{\eta},\overline{\xi})\times h_g(\overline{\zeta},\overline{\eta})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} h_{fg}(\overline{\zeta},\overline{\xi}), \]

i.e. a map

\[ \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{F}(T)}(\eta,f^*(\xi))\times\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{F}(U)}(\zeta,g^*(\eta)) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{F}(U)}(\zeta,(fg)^*(\xi)), \]

by the formula

\[ u\circ v=c_{f,g}(\xi)\cdot g^*(u)\cdot v, \]

i.e. \(u\circ v\) is the composite of the sequence

\[ \zeta\xrightarrow{u} g^*(\eta)\xrightarrow{g^*(u)} g^*f^*(\xi)\xrightarrow{c_{f,g}(\xi)} (fg)^*(\xi). \]

We shall set on the other hand

\[ h(\overline{\eta},\overline{\xi})=\underset{f\in\operatorname{Hom}(T,S)}\coprod h_f(\overline{\eta},\overline{\xi}), \]

and the preceding pairings define pairings

\[ h(\overline{\eta},\overline{\xi})\times h(\overline{\zeta},\overline{\eta})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} h(\overline{\zeta},\overline{\xi}), \]

while the definition of the \(h(\overline{\eta},\overline{\xi})\) implies an obvious map:

\[ p_{\overline{\eta},\overline{\xi}}\colon h(\overline{\eta},\overline{\xi})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}(T,S). \]

This said, one verifies the following points:

  1. 1)

    The composition among elements of the \(h(\overline{\eta},\overline{\xi})\) is associative.

  2. 2)

    For every \(\overline{\xi}=(\xi,S)\) in \(\mathscr{F}_\circ\), consider the identity element of

    \[ h_{\mathrm{id}_S}(\overline{\xi},\overline{\xi})= \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{F}(S)}(\mathrm{id}_S^*(\xi),\xi)=\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{F}(S)}(\xi,\xi), \]

    and its image in \(h(\overline{\xi},\overline{\xi})\). This object is a left and right unit for the composition among elements of the \(h(\overline{\eta},\overline{\xi})\).

    This already shows that one obtains a category \(\mathscr{F}\), by setting

    \[ \operatorname{Ob}\mathscr{F}=\mathscr{F}_\circ,\quad \operatorname{Fl}{\mathscr{F}}= \underset{\overline{\xi},\overline{\eta}\in\mathscr{F}_\circ} \coprod h(\overline{\eta},\overline{\xi}). \]

    (N.B. one cannot simply take for \(\operatorname{Fl}\mathscr{F}\) the union of the sets \(h(\overline{\eta},\overline{\xi})\), for these latter are not necessarily disjoint). Moreover:

  3. 3)

    The maps \(p_\circ\colon \operatorname{Ob}\mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Ob}\mathscr{E}\) and \(p_1=(p_{\overline{\eta},\overline{\xi}})\colon\operatorname{Fl}\mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Fl}{\mathscr{E}}\) define a functor \(p\colon\mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{E}\). In this way, \(\mathscr{F}\) becomes a category over \(\mathscr{E}\); moreover the obvious map \(h_f(\overline{\eta},\overline{\xi})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Hom}(\overline{\eta},\overline{\xi})\) induces a bijection

    \[ h_f(\overline{\eta},\overline{\xi})\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_f(\overline{\eta},\overline{\xi}). \]
  4. 4)

    The obvious maps

    \[ \operatorname{Ob}\mathscr{F}(S)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{F}_\circ=\operatorname{Ob}\mathscr{F},\qquad \operatorname{Fl}\mathscr{F}(S)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Fl}\mathscr{F}, \]

    where the second is defined by the obvious maps

    \[ \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{F}(S)}(\xi,\xi')= h_{\mathrm{id}_S}(\overline{\xi},\overline{\xi}')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}(\overline{\xi},\overline{\xi}') \]

    define an isomorphism

    \[ i_S \colon \mathscr{F}(S)\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow}\mathscr{F}_S. \]
  5. 5)

    For every object \(\overline{\xi}=(S,\xi)\) of \(\mathscr{F}\), and every morphism \(f\colon T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) of \(\mathscr{E}\), consider the element \(\overline{\eta}=(T,\eta)\) of \(\mathscr{F}_T\), with \(\eta=f^*(\xi)\), and the element \(\alpha_f(\xi)\) of \(\operatorname{Hom}(\overline{\eta},\overline{\xi})\), the image of \(\mathrm{id}_{f^*(\xi)}\) under the morphism \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{F}(T)}(f^*(\xi),f^*(\xi))=h_f(\overline{\eta},\overline{\xi}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_f(\overline{\eta},\overline{\xi})\). This element is cartesian, and it is the identity in \(\overline{\xi}\) if \(f=\mathrm{id}_S\); in other words, the set of the \(\alpha_f(\xi)\) defines a normalized cleavage of \(\mathscr{F}\) over \(\mathscr{E}\). Moreover, by construction, one has commutativity in the diagram of functors

    \[ \xymatrix{{\mathscr{F}(S)}\ar[r]^{f^*}\ar[d]_{i_S}&{\mathscr{F}(T)}\ar[d]^{i_T}\\{\mathscr{F}_S}\ar[r]^{f^*_{\mathscr{F}}}&{\mathscr{F}_T}} \]

    where \(f^*_{\mathscr{F}}\) is the inverse image functor by \(f\), relative to the cleavage considered on \(\mathscr{F}\). Finally:

  6. 6)

    the homomorphisms \(c_{f,g}\) given with the pseudo-functor are transformed, by the isomorphisms \(i_S\), into the functorial homomorphisms \(c_{f,g}\) associated with the cleavage of \(\mathscr{F}\).

We confine ourselves to giving the verification of 1) (which is, if anything, less trivial than the others). It suffices to prove the associativity of the composition among the objects of sets of the form \(h_f(\overline{\eta},\overline{\xi})\). Let us therefore consider in \(\mathscr{E}\) morphisms

\[ S\xleftarrow{f} T\xleftarrow{g} U\xleftarrow{h} V \]

and objects

\[ \xi,\ \eta,\ \zeta,\ \tau \]

in \(\mathscr{F}(S),\mathscr{F}(T),\mathscr{F}(U),\mathscr{F}(V)\), finally elements

\[ \begin{array}{ccc} u\in h_f(\overline{\eta},\overline{\xi})&=&\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{F}(T)}(\eta,f^*(\xi))\\ v\in h_g(\overline{\zeta},\overline{\eta})&=&\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{F}(U)}(\zeta,g^*(\eta))\\ w\in h_h(\overline{\tau},\overline{\zeta})&=&\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{F}(V)}(\tau,h^*(\zeta)). \end{array} \]

One wishes to prove the formula

\[ (u\circ v)\circ w = u\circ (v\circ w), \]

which is an equality in \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{F}(V)}(\tau,(fgh)^*(\xi))\). By virtue of the definitions the two sides of this equality are obtained by composition along the upper and lower contour of the diagram below:

\[ \xymatrix{ \tau \ar[r]^w \ar[drr]_{v\circ w} & h^*(\zeta) \ar[r]^{h^*(v)} \ar@<-2pt> `u[rrrrr] `[rrrrr]^{h^*(u\circ v)} [rrrrr] & h^*g^*(\eta) \ar[rr]^{h^*g^*(u)} \ar[d]_{c_{g,h}(\eta)} & & h^*g^*f^*(\xi) \ar[rr]^{h^*(c_{f,g}(\xi))} \ar[d]^{c_{g,h}(f^*(\xi))} & & h^*(fg)^*(\xi) \ar[d]^{c_{fg,h}(\xi)} \\ & & (gh)^*(\eta) \ar[rr]^{(gh)^*(u)} & & (gh)^*f^*(\xi) \ar[rr]^{c_{f,gh}(\xi)} & & (fgh)^*(\xi) } \]

Now the middle square is commutative because \(c_{g,h}\) is a functorial homomorphism, and the right-hand square is commutative by virtue of condition \(B)\) for a pseudo-functor. Whence the announced result.

Of course, it remains to make precise, when the pseudo-functor under consideration already comes from a normalized cleaved category \(\mathscr{F}'\) over \(\mathscr{E}\), how one obtains a natural isomorphism between \(\mathscr{F}'\) and \(\mathscr{F}\). We leave the details to the reader.

We likewise leave to the reader the task of interpreting, in terms of pseudo-functors, the notion of the inverse image of a cleaved category \(\mathscr{F}\) over \(\mathscr{E}\) by a base-change functor \(\mathscr{E}' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{E}\).

blocks.html: 8 Cleaved category defined by a pseudo-functor \(\mathscr{E}^\circ\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathbf{Cat}\)

Let us call, for brevity, a pseudo-functor from \(\mathscr{E}^\circ\) into \(\mathbf{Cat}\) (one ought to say, normalized pseudo-functor), a set of data a), b), c) as above, satisfying the conditions \(A')\) and \(B)\). We set
\[ \mathscr{F}_\circ=\underset{S\in\operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{E})}\coprod \operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{F}(S)), \]

the sum set of the sets \(\operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{F}(S))\) (N.B. here we shall write \(\mathscr{F}(S)\) and not \(\mathscr{F}_S\) for the value at the object \(S\) of \(\mathscr{E}\) of the given pseudo-functor, in order to avoid notational confusions in what follows). \[ p_\circ\colon\mathscr{F}_\circ\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Ob}\mathscr{E}. \]
Let
\[ \overline{\xi} =(S,\xi),\quad \overline{\eta}=(T,\eta)\qquad\qquad \text{(with $\xi\in\operatorname{Ob}\mathscr{F}(S)$, $\eta\in\operatorname{Ob}\mathscr{F}(T)$)} \]
be two elements of \(\mathscr{F}_\circ\), and let \(f\in\operatorname{Hom}(T,S)\); we shall set
\[ h_f(\overline{\eta},\overline{\xi})=\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{F}(T)}(\eta,f^*(\xi)). \]

If we have moreover a morphism \(g\colon U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T\) in \(\mathscr{E}\), and a \(\zeta\in\operatorname{Ob}\mathscr{F}(U)\), we define a map, denoted \((u,v)\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} u\circ v\):
\[ h_f(\overline{\eta},\overline{\xi})\times h_g(\overline{\zeta},\overline{\eta})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} h_{fg}(\overline{\zeta},\overline{\xi}), \]
i.e. a map

\[ \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{F}(T)}(\eta,f^*(\xi))\times\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{F}(U)}(\zeta,g^*(\eta)) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{F}(U)}(\zeta,(fg)^*(\xi)), \]
by the formula
\[ u\circ v=c_{f,g}(\xi)\cdot g^*(u)\cdot v, \]
i.e. \(u\circ v\) is the composite of the sequence
\[ \zeta\xrightarrow{u} g^*(\eta)\xrightarrow{g^*(u)} g^*f^*(\xi)\xrightarrow{c_{f,g}(\xi)} (fg)^*(\xi). \]
We shall set on the other hand
\[ h(\overline{\eta},\overline{\xi})=\underset{f\in\operatorname{Hom}(T,S)}\coprod h_f(\overline{\eta},\overline{\xi}), \]
and the preceding pairings define pairings
\[ h(\overline{\eta},\overline{\xi})\times h(\overline{\zeta},\overline{\eta})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} h(\overline{\zeta},\overline{\xi}), \]
while the definition of the \(h(\overline{\eta},\overline{\xi})\) implies an obvious map:
\[ p_{\overline{\eta},\overline{\xi}}\colon h(\overline{\eta},\overline{\xi})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}(T,S). \]
This said, one verifies the following points:

1. 1)

    The composition among elements of the \(h(\overline{\eta},\overline{\xi})\) is associative.

  2. 2)

    For every \(\overline{\xi}=(\xi,S)\) in \(\mathscr{F}_\circ\), consider the identity element of

    \[ h_{\mathrm{id}_S}(\overline{\xi},\overline{\xi})= \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{F}(S)}(\mathrm{id}_S^*(\xi),\xi)=\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{F}(S)}(\xi,\xi), \]

    and its image in \(h(\overline{\xi},\overline{\xi})\). This object is a left and right unit for the composition among elements of the \(h(\overline{\eta},\overline{\xi})\).

    This already shows that one obtains a category \(\mathscr{F}\), by setting

    \[ \operatorname{Ob}\mathscr{F}=\mathscr{F}_\circ,\quad \operatorname{Fl}{\mathscr{F}}= \underset{\overline{\xi},\overline{\eta}\in\mathscr{F}_\circ} \coprod h(\overline{\eta},\overline{\xi}). \]

    (N.B. one cannot simply take for \(\operatorname{Fl}\mathscr{F}\) the union of the sets \(h(\overline{\eta},\overline{\xi})\), for these latter are not necessarily disjoint). Moreover:

  3. 3)

    The maps \(p_\circ\colon \operatorname{Ob}\mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Ob}\mathscr{E}\) and \(p_1=(p_{\overline{\eta},\overline{\xi}})\colon\operatorname{Fl}\mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Fl}{\mathscr{E}}\) define a functor \(p\colon\mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{E}\). In this way, \(\mathscr{F}\) becomes a category over \(\mathscr{E}\); moreover the obvious map \(h_f(\overline{\eta},\overline{\xi})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Hom}(\overline{\eta},\overline{\xi})\) induces a bijection

    \[ h_f(\overline{\eta},\overline{\xi})\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_f(\overline{\eta},\overline{\xi}). \]
  4. 4)

    The obvious maps

    \[ \operatorname{Ob}\mathscr{F}(S)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{F}_\circ=\operatorname{Ob}\mathscr{F},\qquad \operatorname{Fl}\mathscr{F}(S)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Fl}\mathscr{F}, \]

    where the second is defined by the obvious maps

    \[ \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{F}(S)}(\xi,\xi')= h_{\mathrm{id}_S}(\overline{\xi},\overline{\xi}')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}(\overline{\xi},\overline{\xi}') \]

    define an isomorphism

    \[ i_S \colon \mathscr{F}(S)\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow}\mathscr{F}_S. \]
  5. 5)

    For every object \(\overline{\xi}=(S,\xi)\) of \(\mathscr{F}\), and every morphism \(f\colon T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) of \(\mathscr{E}\), consider the element \(\overline{\eta}=(T,\eta)\) of \(\mathscr{F}_T\), with \(\eta=f^*(\xi)\), and the element \(\alpha_f(\xi)\) of \(\operatorname{Hom}(\overline{\eta},\overline{\xi})\), the image of \(\mathrm{id}_{f^*(\xi)}\) under the morphism \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{F}(T)}(f^*(\xi),f^*(\xi))=h_f(\overline{\eta},\overline{\xi}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_f(\overline{\eta},\overline{\xi})\). This element is cartesian, and it is the identity in \(\overline{\xi}\) if \(f=\mathrm{id}_S\); in other words, the set of the \(\alpha_f(\xi)\) defines a normalized cleavage of \(\mathscr{F}\) over \(\mathscr{E}\). Moreover, by construction, one has commutativity in the diagram of functors

    \[ \xymatrix{{\mathscr{F}(S)}\ar[r]^{f^*}\ar[d]_{i_S}&{\mathscr{F}(T)}\ar[d]^{i_T}\\{\mathscr{F}_S}\ar[r]^{f^*_{\mathscr{F}}}&{\mathscr{F}_T}} \]

    where \(f^*_{\mathscr{F}}\) is the inverse image functor by \(f\), relative to the cleavage considered on \(\mathscr{F}\). Finally:

  6. 6)

    the homomorphisms \(c_{f,g}\) given with the pseudo-functor are transformed, by the isomorphisms \(i_S\), into the functorial homomorphisms \(c_{f,g}\) associated with the cleavage of \(\mathscr{F}\).

We confine ourselves to giving the verification of 1) (which is, if anything, less trivial than the others). It suffices to prove the associativity of the composition among the objects of sets of the form \(h_f(\overline{\eta},\overline{\xi})\). Let us therefore consider in \(\mathscr{E}\) morphisms
\[ S\xleftarrow{f} T\xleftarrow{g} U\xleftarrow{h} V \]
and objects
\[ \xi,\ \eta,\ \zeta,\ \tau \]
in \(\mathscr{F}(S),\mathscr{F}(T),\mathscr{F}(U),\mathscr{F}(V)\), finally elements

\[ \begin{array}{ccc} u\in h_f(\overline{\eta},\overline{\xi})&=&\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{F}(T)}(\eta,f^*(\xi))\\ v\in h_g(\overline{\zeta},\overline{\eta})&=&\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{F}(U)}(\zeta,g^*(\eta))\\ w\in h_h(\overline{\tau},\overline{\zeta})&=&\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{F}(V)}(\tau,h^*(\zeta)). \end{array} \]
One wishes to prove the formula
\[ (u\circ v)\circ w = u\circ (v\circ w), \]

which is an equality in \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{F}(V)}(\tau,(fgh)^*(\xi))\). By virtue of the definitions the two sides of this equality are obtained by composition along the upper and lower contour of the diagram below:

\[ \xymatrix{ \tau \ar[r]^w \ar[drr]_{v\circ w} & h^*(\zeta) \ar[r]^{h^*(v)} \ar@<-2pt> `u[rrrrr] `[rrrrr]^{h^*(u\circ v)} [rrrrr] & h^*g^*(\eta) \ar[rr]^{h^*g^*(u)} \ar[d]_{c_{g,h}(\eta)} & & h^*g^*f^*(\xi) \ar[rr]^{h^*(c_{f,g}(\xi))} \ar[d]^{c_{g,h}(f^*(\xi))} & & h^*(fg)^*(\xi) \ar[d]^{c_{fg,h}(\xi)} \\ & & (gh)^*(\eta) \ar[rr]^{(gh)^*(u)} & & (gh)^*f^*(\xi) \ar[rr]^{c_{f,gh}(\xi)} & & (fgh)^*(\xi) } \]

Now the middle square is commutative because \(c_{g,h}\) is a functorial homomorphism, and the right-hand square is commutative by virtue of condition \(B)\) for a pseudo-functor. Whence the announced result.

Of course, it remains to make precise, when the pseudo-functor under consideration already comes from a normalized cleaved category \(\mathscr{F}'\) over \(\mathscr{E}\), how one obtains a natural isomorphism between \(\mathscr{F}'\) and \(\mathscr{F}\). We leave the details to the reader.

We likewise leave to the reader the task of interpreting, in terms of pseudo-functors, the notion of the inverse image of a cleaved category \(\mathscr{F}\) over \(\mathscr{E}\) by a base-change functor \(\mathscr{E}' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{E}\).

pages.title:
Example: cleaved category defined by a functor
\(\mathscr{E}^\circ\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathbf{Cat}\); split categories over \(\mathscr{E}\)
pages.html:
9 Example: cleaved category defined by a functor \(\mathscr{E}^\circ\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathbf{Cat}\); split categories over \(\mathscr{E}\)

Suppose that we have a functor

\[ \varphi\colon\mathscr{E}^\circ\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathbf{Cat}, \]

it then defines a pseudo-functor by setting

\[ \mathscr{F}(S)=\varphi(S), \quad f^*=\varphi(f),\quad c_{f,g}=\mathrm{id}_{(fg)^*} \]

Thus the construction of the preceding article gives us a category \(\mathscr{F}\) cleaved over \(\mathscr{E}\), said to be associated with the functor \(\varphi\). For a category cleaved over \(\mathscr{E}\) to be isomorphic to a cleaved category defined by a functor \(\varphi:\mathscr{E}^\circ\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathbf{Cat}\), it is manifestly necessary and sufficient that it satisfy the conditions:

\[ (fg)^*=g^*f^*,\quad c_{f,g}=\mathrm{id}_{(fg)^*}. \]

In terms of the set \(K\) of transport morphisms, this also means simply that the composite of two transport morphisms is a transport morphism. A cleavage of a category \(\mathscr{F}\) over \(\mathscr{E}\) satisfying the preceding condition is called a splitting of \(\mathscr{F}\) over \(\mathscr{E}\), and a category \(\mathscr{F}\) over \(\mathscr{E}\) equipped with a splitting is called a split category over \(\mathscr{E}\). It is therefore a particular case of the notion of cleaved category. The category of split categories over \(\mathscr{E}\) is therefore equivalent to \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}(\mathscr{E}^\circ,\mathbf{Cat})\). Note that a category split over \(\mathscr{E}\) is a fortiori a category cleaved over \(\mathscr{E}\).

If \(\mathscr{F}\) is a category fibred over \(\mathscr{E}\), there does not always exist a splitting on \(\mathscr{F}\). Suppose for example that \(\operatorname{Ob}\mathscr{E}\) and \(\operatorname{Ob}\mathscr{F}\) are reduced to a single element, and that the set of endomorphisms of the said element is a group \(E\) resp. \(F\), so that the projection functor \(p\) is given by a homomorphism of groups \(p\colon F\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} E\), surjective since \(p\) is fibrant. One then verifies at once that the set of cleavages of \(\mathscr{F}\) over \(\mathscr{E}\) is in one-to-one correspondence with the set of maps \(s\colon E\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F\) such that \(ps=\mathrm{id}_E\) (i.e. the set of “systems of representatives” for the classes mod the subgroup \(G\) kernel of the surjective homomorphism \(p\colon F\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} E\)). A cleavage is a splitting if and only if \(s\) is a homomorphism of groups. To say that there exists a splitting therefore means that the extension of groups \(F\) of \(E\) by \(G\) is trivial, which is expressed, when \(G\) is commutative, by the vanishing of a certain cohomology class in \(\mathrm{H}^2(E,G)\) (where \(G\) is regarded as a group on which \(E\) operates).

Suppose however that \(\mathscr{F}\) is a category fibred over \(\mathscr{E}\) such that the \(\mathscr{F}_S\) are rigid categories, i.e. the group of automorphisms of every object of \(\mathscr{F}_S\) is reduced to the identity. It is then easy to prove that \(\mathscr{F}\) admits a splitting over \(\mathscr{E}\). Indeed, one observes first that the question of existence of a splitting is not modified if one replaces \(\mathscr{F}\) by an \(\mathscr{E}\)-equivalent category, which brings us in the present case to the case where the \(\mathscr{F}_S\) are rigid and reduced categories (i.e. two objects isomorphic in \(\mathscr{F}_S\) are identical). But if \(G\) is a rigid and reduced category, every isomorphism of two functors \(H\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G\) (where \(H\) is an arbitrary category) is an identity. It follows that if \(\mathscr{F}\) is a category fibred over \(\mathscr{E}\), such that the fibre-categories are rigid and reduced, then there exists a unique cleavage of \(\mathscr{F}\) over \(\mathscr{E}\), which is necessarily a splitting. Hence \(\mathscr{F}\) is isomorphic to the category defined by a functor \(\varphi\colon \mathscr{E}^\circ\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbf{Cat}\), such that the \(\varphi(S)\) are rigid and discrete categories, and the functor \(\varphi\) is defined up to isomorphism.

pages.title: Co-fibred categories, bi-fibred categories
pages.html:
10 Co-fibred categories, bi-fibred categories

Consider a category \(\mathscr{F}\) over \(\mathscr{E}\), with the projection functor

\[ p\colon \mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{E}, \]

it defines a category \(\mathscr{F}^\circ\) over \(\mathscr{E}^\circ\), by the projection functor

\[ p^\circ\colon \mathscr{F}^\circ\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{E}^\circ. \]

A morphism \(\alpha\colon \eta\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\xi\) in \(\mathscr{F}\) is said to be co-cartesian if it is a cartesian morphism for \(\mathscr{F}^\circ\) over \(\mathscr{E}^\circ\). Making this explicit, one sees that it means that for every object \(\xi'\) of \(\mathscr{F}_S\), the map \(u\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} u\circ \alpha\)

\[ \operatorname{Hom}_S(\xi,\xi')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Hom}_f(\eta,\xi') \]

is bijective. One then also says that \((\xi,\alpha)\) is a direct image of \(\eta\) by \(f\), in the category \(\mathscr{F}\) over \(\mathscr{E}\). If it exists for every \(\eta\) in \(\mathscr{F}_T\), one says that the direct image functor by \(f\) exists, and one denotes this functor by \(f_*^{\mathscr{F}}\) or \(f_*\), once one is chosen. It is thus defined by an isomorphism of bifunctors on \(\mathscr{F}_T^\circ\times\mathscr{F}_S\):

\[ \operatorname{Hom}_S(f_*(\eta),\xi)\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_f(\eta,\xi). \]

Thus if \(f_*\) exists, in order for \(f^*\) to exist, it is necessary and sufficient that \(f_*\) admit an adjoint functor, i.e. that there exist a functor \(f^*\colon \mathscr{F}_S\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{F}_T\) and an isomorphism of bifunctors

\[ \operatorname{Hom}_S(f_*(\eta),\xi)\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_T(\eta,f^*(\xi)). \]

Let \(g\colon U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T\) be another morphism in \(\mathscr{E}\), and suppose that the inverse and direct images by \(f,g\) and \(fg\) exist. Consider then the functorial homomorphisms

\[ \begin{array}{cccc} c^{f,g}\colon&f_*g_*&\displaystyle\mathchoice{\longleftarrow}{\leftarrow}{\leftarrow}{\leftarrow}&(fg)_*\\ c_{f,g}\colon&g^*f^*&\displaystyle\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}&(fg)^*. \end{array} \]

One observes that if one regards \(f_*g_*\) and \(g^*f^*\) as a pair of adjoint functors, as well as \((fg)_*\) and \((fg)^*\), the two preceding homomorphisms are adjoint to one another. Hence one is an isomorphism if and only if the other is. In particular:

Proposition 1

Suppose that the category \(\mathscr{F}\) over \(\mathscr{E}\) is prefibred and co-prefibred. In order for it to be fibred, it is necessary and sufficient that it be co-fibred.

Of course, one says that \(\mathscr{F}\) is co-prefibred resp. co-fibred over \(\mathscr{E}\), if \(\mathscr{F}^\circ\) is prefibred resp. fibred over \(\mathscr{E}\). We shall say that \(\mathscr{F}\) is bi-fibred over \(\mathscr{E}\), if it is at once fibred and co-fibred over \(\mathscr{E}\).

blocks.html: 10 Co-fibred categories, bi-fibred categories

\[ p\colon \mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{E}, \]

blocks.html:
Proposition 1
