pages.title: Various examples
pages.html:
11 Various examples

  1. a)

    Categories of arrows of \(\mathscr{E}\). Let \(\mathscr{E}\) be a category. Let us denote by \(\mathbf{\Delta^1}\) the category associated with the totally ordered set with two elements \([0,1]\); it thus has two objects 0 and 1, and, besides the two identity morphisms, an arrow \((0,1)\) with source 0 and target 1. Let

    \[ \operatorname{\mathbf{Fl}}(\mathscr{E})=\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}(\mathbf{\Delta^1},\mathscr{E}) \]

    we call it the category of arrows of \(\mathscr{E}\). The object 1 of \(\mathbf{\Delta^1}\) defines a canonical functor, called the target-functor

    \[ \operatorname{\mathbf{Fl}}(\mathscr{E})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{E} \]

    (the functor defined by the object 0 of \(\mathbf{\Delta^1}\) is called the source-functor). For every object \(S\) of \(\mathscr{E}\), the fibre-category \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Fl}}(\mathscr{E})_S\) is canonically isomorphic to the category \(\mathscr{E}_{/S}\) of objects of \(\mathscr{E}\) over \(S\).

    Consider a morphism \(f\colon T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) in \(\mathscr{E}\); then to it corresponds a canonical functor

    \[ f_*\colon \mathscr{E}_{/T}=\mathscr{F}_T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{E}_{/S}=\mathscr{F}_S \]

    and a functorial isomorphism

    \[ \operatorname{Hom}_S(f_*(\eta),\xi)\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow}\operatorname{Hom}_f(\eta,\xi) \]

    which thus makes \(f_*\) a direct image functor for \(f\) in \(\mathscr{F}\). Here we have moreover

    \[ (\mathrm{id}_S)_*=\mathrm{id}_{\mathscr{F}_S},\quad (fg)_*=f_*g_*,\quad c^{f,g}=\mathrm{id}_{(fg)}, \]

    i.e. \(\mathscr{F}\) is endowed with a co-splitting over \(\mathscr{E}\). A fortiori, \(\mathscr{F}\) is co-fibred over \(\mathscr{E}\). Let us now note that the set of morphisms in \(\mathscr{F}\) is in one-to-one correspondence with the set of commutative square diagrams in \(\mathscr{E}\).

    \[ \xymatrix{{X}\ar[d]_-u&{Y}\ar[l]_-{f'}\ar[d]^-v\\{S}&{T}\ar[l]_-{f}} \]

    By definition, the morphism in question is cartesian if the square is cartesian in \(\mathscr{E}\), i.e. if it makes \(Y\) a fibred product of \(X\) and \(T\) over \(S\). The inverse image functor \(f^*\) thus exists if and only if for every object \(X\) over \(S\), the fibred product \(X\times_ST\) exists. It follows from 1 that if the product of two objects over a third always exists in \(\mathscr{E}\), i.e. if \(\mathscr{F}\) is prefibred over \(\mathscr{E}\), then \(\mathscr{F}\) is even fibred over \(\mathscr{E}\).

  2. b)

    Category of presheaves or sheaves on variable spaces

    Let \(\mathscr{E}=\mathbf{Top}\) be the category of topological spaces. If \(T\) is a topological space, we shall denote by \(\mathscr{U}(T)\) the category of open sets of \(T\), where the morphisms are the inclusion maps. If \(\mathscr{C}\) is a category, a functor \(\mathscr{U}(T)^\circ\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{C}\) is called a presheaf on \(T\) with values in \(\mathscr{C}\), and a sheaf if it satisfies a left exactness condition that we do not repeat here. The category \(\mathscr{P}(T)\) of presheaves on \(T\) with values in \(\mathscr{C}\) is by definition the category \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}(\mathscr{U}(T)^\circ,\mathscr{C})\), and the category \(\mathscr{F}(T)\) of sheaves on \(T\) with values in \(\mathscr{C}\) is the full subcategory whose objects are the objects of \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}(\mathscr{U}(T)^\circ,\mathscr{C})\) that are sheaves. If \(f\colon T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is a morphism in \(\mathscr{E}\), i.e. a continuous map of topological spaces, to it corresponds, by the increasing map \(U\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} f^{-1}(U)\), a functor \(\mathscr{U}(S)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{U}(T)\), whence a functor

    \[ f_*\colon\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}(\mathscr{U}(T)^\circ,\mathscr{C})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}(\mathscr{U}(S)^\circ,\mathscr{C}) \]

    called the direct image functor of presheaves by \(f\). One sees at once that the direct image of a sheaf is a sheaf, so the functor \(f_*\colon \mathscr{P}(T)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{P}(S)\) induces a functor, also denoted \(f_*\colon\mathscr{F}(T)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{F}(S)\). One moreover verifies trivially (by the associativity of the composition of functors) that one has, for a second continuous map \(g\colon U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T\), the identity

    \[ (gf)_* =g_*f_*,\qquad \text{likewise }\ (\mathrm{id}_S)_*=\mathrm{id}_{\mathscr{P}(S)}. \]

    In this way, one has obtained a functor

    \[ S\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \mathscr{P}(S) \]

    resp.

    \[ S\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \mathscr{F}(S) \]

    from \(\mathscr{E}\) to \(\mathbf{Cat}\). In fact, we are interested in the corresponding functor

    \[ S\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \mathscr{P}(S)^\circ,\qquad\text{resp. ~}S\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \mathscr{F}(S)^\circ. \]

    It defines a co-fibred category, and even co-split, over the category of topological spaces, which one calls the co-fibred category of presheaves (resp. sheaves) with values in \(\mathscr{C}\) (understood: over variable spaces). Making explicit the construction of 8, one sees that a morphism from a presheaf \(B\) on \(T\) to a presheaf \(A\) on \(S\) is a pair \((f,u)\) formed of a continuous map from \(T\) to \(S\), and of a morphism \(u\colon A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} f_*(B)\) in the category \(\mathscr{P}(S)\). This description holds equally for morphisms of sheaves, \(\mathscr{F}\) being a full subcategory of \(\mathscr{P}\).

    In the most important cases, the category \(\mathscr{P}\) and the category \(\mathscr{F}\) over \(\mathscr{E}\) are also fibred categories, i.e. for every continuous map, the direct image functors \(\mathscr{P}(T)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{P}(S)\) and \(\mathscr{F}(T)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{F}(S)\) have an adjoint functor, which is then denoted \(f^*\) and called the inverse image functor of presheaves resp. the inverse image functor of sheaves, by the continuous map \(f\). This functor exists for example if \(\mathscr{C}=\mathbf{Ens}\). One can show that the functor \(f^*\colon \mathscr{P}(S)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{P}(T)\) exists whenever in \(\mathscr{C}\) the direct limits (relative to diagrams in the Universe considered) exist. The question is less easy for \(\mathscr{F}\); one will note indeed that (even in the case \(\mathscr{C}=\mathbf{Ens}\)) the inverse image of a presheaf which is a sheaf is in general not a sheaf, in other words the inverse image functor of sheaves is not isomorphic to the functor induced by the inverse image functor of presheaves (despite the common notation \(f^*\)). Thus, \(\mathscr{F}\) is a co-fibred subcategory of \(\mathscr{P}\), but not a fibred subcategory, i.e. the inclusion functor \(\mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{P}\) is not fibrant.

    The co-fibred category \(\mathscr{P}\) can be deduced from a more general co-fibred (or rather fibred) category, obtained as follows. For every category \(\mathscr{U}\) (in the fixed Universe), one sets

    \[ \mathscr{P}(\mathscr{U})=\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}(\mathscr{U},\mathscr{C}) \]

    and one notes that \(\mathscr{U}\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}\mathscr{P}(\mathscr{U})\) is in a natural way a contravariant functor in \(\mathscr{U}\), from the category \(\mathbf{Cat}\) to \(\mathbf{Cat}\). It thus defines a split category over \(\mathscr{E}=\mathbf{Cat}\), which we shall denote \(\mathbf{Cat}_{/\!/\mathscr{C}}\). The objects of this category are the pairs \((\mathscr{U},p)\) of a category \(\mathscr{U}\) and a functor \(p\colon\mathscr{U}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{C}\), and a morphism from \((\mathscr{U},p)\) to \((\mathscr{V},q)\) is essentially a pair \((f,u)\), where \(f\) is a functor \(\mathscr{U}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{V}\) and \(u\) a homomorphism of functors \(u\colon p\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} qf\). We leave to the reader the task of making explicit the composition of morphisms in \(\mathbf{Cat}_{/\!/\mathscr{C}}\). The projection-functor

    \[ \mathscr{F}=\mathbf{Cat}_{/\!/\mathscr{C}}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{E}=\mathbf{Cat} \]

    associates to the pair \((\mathscr{U},p)\) the object \(\mathscr{U}\); the fibre-category at \(\mathscr{U}\) is the category \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}(\mathscr{U},\mathscr{C})\) (up to isomorphism). When in \(\mathscr{C}\) the direct limits exist, one shows easily that the fibred category \(\mathbf{Cat}_{/\!/\mathscr{C}}\) over \(\mathbf{Cat}\) is also co-fibred over \(\mathbf{Cat}\), i.e. one can define the notion of direct image of a functor \(p\colon\mathscr{U}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{C}\) by a functor \(f\colon\mathscr{U}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{V}\). The category of presheaves is deduced from the preceding fibred category by the base change

    \[ \mathbf{Top}^\circ\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathbf{Cat} \]

    (functor \(S\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}\mathscr{U}(S)\) defined above), which gives a fibred category over \(\mathbf{Top}^\circ\), and passing to the opposite category, one obtains the co-fibred category \(\mathscr{P}\) of presheaves over \(\mathbf{Top}\). The notion of inverse image of a functor corresponds to that of direct image of a presheaf, the notion of direct image of a functor to that of inverse image of a presheaf.

  3. c)

    Objects with operators over an object with operators

    Let \(\mathscr{F}\) be a category over \(\mathscr{E}\), and let \(S\) be an object of \(\mathscr{E}\) on which a group \(G\) operates, on the left to fix ideas. This object with operators can be interpreted as corresponding to a functor \(\lambda\colon\mathscr{E}'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{E}\) from the category (with a single object, having \(G\) as group of endomorphisms) \(\mathscr{E}'\) defined by \(G\), to the category \(\mathscr{E}\), and thus defines by base change a category \(\mathscr{F}'\) over \(\mathscr{E}'\), which is fibred resp. co-fibred when \(\mathscr{F}\) is so over \(\mathscr{E}\). A section of \(\mathscr{E}'\) over \(\mathscr{F}'\) (necessarily cartesian, since \(\mathscr{E}'\) is a groupoid, and every isomorphism in \(\mathscr{F}'\) is cartesian by virtue of 12) can also be interpreted as an \(\mathscr{E}\)-functor \(\mathscr{E}'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{F}\) over \(\lambda\), or also as an object with operators \(\xi\) in \(\mathscr{F}\) “over” the object with operators \(S\).

  4. d)

    Pairs of quasi-inverse adjoint functors; self-dualities

    When the base-category \(\mathscr{E}\) is reduced to two objects \(a,b\) and, besides the identity arrows, to two isomorphisms \(f\colon a\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} b\) and \(g\colon b\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} a\) inverse to one another (i.e. \(\mathscr{E}\) is a connected rigid groupoid with two objects), a normalized cleft category over \(\mathscr{E}\) is essentially the same thing as the system formed by two categories \(\mathscr{F}_a\) and \(\mathscr{F}_b\) and a pair of adjoint functors \(G\colon \mathscr{F}_a\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{F}_b\) and \(F\colon \mathscr{F}_b\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{F}_a\), which are equivalences of categories (hence quasi-inverse to one another). One takes for \(\mathscr{F}_a\) and \(\mathscr{F}_b\) the fibre categories of \(\mathscr{F}\), for \(F\) and \(G\) the functors \(f^*\) and \(g^*\), and the two isomorphisms

    \[ u\colon FG\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow}\mathrm{id}_{\mathscr{F}_a}\qquad v\colon GF\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow}\mathrm{id}_{\mathscr{F}_b} \]

    are \(c_{g,f}\) and \(c_{f,g}\). The two usual conditions of compatibility between \(u\) and \(v\) are none other than condition 4 \(B)\) for the composites \(fgf\) and \(gfg\). It is easy to show that these conditions suffice to imply that one indeed has a pseudo-functor \(\mathscr{E}^\circ\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathbf{Cat}\).

    An interesting case is that in which one has

    \[ \mathscr{F}_b=\mathscr{F}_a^\circ,\quad G=F^\circ,\quad v=u^\circ. \]

    One calls self-duality in a category \(\mathscr{C}\) the datum of a functor \(D\colon \mathscr{C}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{C}^\circ\), and of an isomorphism \(u\colon DD^\circ\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow}\mathrm{id}_{\mathscr{C}}\), such that \(u\) and the isomorphism \(u^\circ\colon D^\circ D\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow}\mathrm{id}_{\mathscr{C}^\circ}\) make \((D,D^\circ)\) a pair of adjoint functors, (necessarily quasi-inverse to one another). This condition is written:

    \[ D(u(x))=u(D(x))\qquad \text{for every $x\in\operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{C})$.} \]
  5. e)

    Categories over a discrete category \(\mathscr{E}\). One says that \(\mathscr{E}\) is a discrete category if every arrow in it is an identity arrow, so that \(\mathscr{E}\) is defined up to unique isomorphism by the knowledge of the set \(I=\operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{E})\). The datum of a category \(\mathscr{F}\) over \(\mathscr{E}\) thus amounts (up to unique isomorphism) to the datum of a family of categories \(\mathscr{F}_i\) (\(i\in I\)), the fibre categories. Every category \(\mathscr{F}\) over \(\mathscr{E}\) is fibred, every \(\mathscr{E}\)-functor \(\mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{G}\) is cartesian, one has a canonical isomorphism

    \[ \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G}) \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \underset{i}\prod\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}(\mathscr{F}_i,\mathscr{G}_i). \]

    In particular, one obtains

    \[ \mathbf{\Gamma}(\mathscr{F}/\mathscr{E})=\varprojlim\mathscr{F}/\mathscr{E}\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow}\underset{i}\prod \mathscr{E}_i. \]
  6. f)

    Suppose that \(\mathscr{E}\) has exactly two objects \(S\) and \(T\), and, besides the identity morphisms, one morphism \(f\colon T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\). Then a category \(\mathscr{F}\) over \(\mathscr{E}\) is defined, up to unique \(\mathscr{E}\)-isomorphism, by the datum of two categories \(\mathscr{F}_S\) and \(\mathscr{F}_T\) and of a bifunctor \(H(\eta,\xi)\) on \(\mathscr{F}_T^\circ\times\mathscr{F}_S\), with values in \(\mathbf{Ens}\). Indeed, if \(\mathscr{F}\) is a category over \(\mathscr{E}\), to it one associates the two fibre-categories \(\mathscr{F}_S\) and \(\mathscr{F}_T\) and the bifunctor \(H(\eta,\xi)=\operatorname{Hom}_f(\eta,\xi)\). We leave to the reader the task of making explicit the construction in the reverse direction. For the category envisaged to be fibred (or prefibred, this comes to the same thing) it is necessary and sufficient that the functor \(H\) be representable with respect to the argument \(\xi\). For it to be co-fibred, it is necessary and sufficient that \(H\) be representable with respect to the argument \(\eta\).

  7. g)

    Let \(\mathscr{F}=\mathscr{C}\times\mathscr{E}\), considered as a category over \(\mathscr{E}\) by means of \(\mathrm{pr}_2\). Then \(\mathscr{F}\) is fibred and co-fibred over \(\mathscr{E}\), and is even endowed with a canonical splitting and co-splitting, corresponding to the constant functor on \(\mathscr{E}\), resp. on \(\mathscr{E}^\circ\), with values in \(\mathbf{Cat}\), of value \(\mathscr{C}\). One has

    \[ \mathbf{\Gamma}(\mathscr{F}/\mathscr{E})\simeq\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}(\mathscr{E},\mathscr{C}) \]

    and \(\varprojlim\mathscr{F}/\mathscr{E}\) corresponds to the full subcategory formed of the functors \(F\colon\mathscr{E\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{C}}\) transforming arbitrary morphisms into isomorphisms.

blocks.html: 11 Various examples

1. a)

    Categories of arrows of \(\mathscr{E}\). Let \(\mathscr{E}\) be a category. Let us denote by \(\mathbf{\Delta^1}\) the category associated with the totally ordered set with two elements \([0,1]\); it thus has two objects 0 and 1, and, besides the two identity morphisms, an arrow \((0,1)\) with source 0 and target 1. Let

    \[ \operatorname{\mathbf{Fl}}(\mathscr{E})=\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}(\mathbf{\Delta^1},\mathscr{E}) \]

    we call it the category of arrows of \(\mathscr{E}\). The object 1 of \(\mathbf{\Delta^1}\) defines a canonical functor, called the target-functor

    \[ \operatorname{\mathbf{Fl}}(\mathscr{E})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{E} \]

    (the functor defined by the object 0 of \(\mathbf{\Delta^1}\) is called the source-functor). For every object \(S\) of \(\mathscr{E}\), the fibre-category \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Fl}}(\mathscr{E})_S\) is canonically isomorphic to the category \(\mathscr{E}_{/S}\) of objects of \(\mathscr{E}\) over \(S\).

    Consider a morphism \(f\colon T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) in \(\mathscr{E}\); then to it corresponds a canonical functor

    \[ f_*\colon \mathscr{E}_{/T}=\mathscr{F}_T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{E}_{/S}=\mathscr{F}_S \]

    and a functorial isomorphism

    \[ \operatorname{Hom}_S(f_*(\eta),\xi)\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow}\operatorname{Hom}_f(\eta,\xi) \]

    which thus makes \(f_*\) a direct image functor for \(f\) in \(\mathscr{F}\). Here we have moreover

    \[ (\mathrm{id}_S)_*=\mathrm{id}_{\mathscr{F}_S},\quad (fg)_*=f_*g_*,\quad c^{f,g}=\mathrm{id}_{(fg)}, \]

    i.e. \(\mathscr{F}\) is endowed with a co-splitting over \(\mathscr{E}\). A fortiori, \(\mathscr{F}\) is co-fibred over \(\mathscr{E}\). Let us now note that the set of morphisms in \(\mathscr{F}\) is in one-to-one correspondence with the set of commutative square diagrams in \(\mathscr{E}\).

    \[ \xymatrix{{X}\ar[d]_-u&{Y}\ar[l]_-{f'}\ar[d]^-v\\{S}&{T}\ar[l]_-{f}} \]

    By definition, the morphism in question is cartesian if the square is cartesian in \(\mathscr{E}\), i.e. if it makes \(Y\) a fibred product of \(X\) and \(T\) over \(S\). The inverse image functor \(f^*\) thus exists if and only if for every object \(X\) over \(S\), the fibred product \(X\times_ST\) exists. It follows from 1 that if the product of two objects over a third always exists in \(\mathscr{E}\), i.e. if \(\mathscr{F}\) is prefibred over \(\mathscr{E}\), then \(\mathscr{F}\) is even fibred over \(\mathscr{E}\).

  2. b)

    Category of presheaves or sheaves on variable spaces

    Let \(\mathscr{E}=\mathbf{Top}\) be the category of topological spaces. If \(T\) is a topological space, we shall denote by \(\mathscr{U}(T)\) the category of open sets of \(T\), where the morphisms are the inclusion maps. If \(\mathscr{C}\) is a category, a functor \(\mathscr{U}(T)^\circ\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{C}\) is called a presheaf on \(T\) with values in \(\mathscr{C}\), and a sheaf if it satisfies a left exactness condition that we do not repeat here. The category \(\mathscr{P}(T)\) of presheaves on \(T\) with values in \(\mathscr{C}\) is by definition the category \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}(\mathscr{U}(T)^\circ,\mathscr{C})\), and the category \(\mathscr{F}(T)\) of sheaves on \(T\) with values in \(\mathscr{C}\) is the full subcategory whose objects are the objects of \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}(\mathscr{U}(T)^\circ,\mathscr{C})\) that are sheaves. If \(f\colon T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is a morphism in \(\mathscr{E}\), i.e. a continuous map of topological spaces, to it corresponds, by the increasing map \(U\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} f^{-1}(U)\), a functor \(\mathscr{U}(S)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{U}(T)\), whence a functor

    \[ f_*\colon\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}(\mathscr{U}(T)^\circ,\mathscr{C})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}(\mathscr{U}(S)^\circ,\mathscr{C}) \]

    called the direct image functor of presheaves by \(f\). One sees at once that the direct image of a sheaf is a sheaf, so the functor \(f_*\colon \mathscr{P}(T)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{P}(S)\) induces a functor, also denoted \(f_*\colon\mathscr{F}(T)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{F}(S)\). One moreover verifies trivially (by the associativity of the composition of functors) that one has, for a second continuous map \(g\colon U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T\), the identity

    \[ (gf)_* =g_*f_*,\qquad \text{likewise }\ (\mathrm{id}_S)_*=\mathrm{id}_{\mathscr{P}(S)}. \]

    In this way, one has obtained a functor

    \[ S\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \mathscr{P}(S) \]

    resp.

    \[ S\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \mathscr{F}(S) \]

    from \(\mathscr{E}\) to \(\mathbf{Cat}\). In fact, we are interested in the corresponding functor

    \[ S\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \mathscr{P}(S)^\circ,\qquad\text{resp. ~}S\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \mathscr{F}(S)^\circ. \]

    It defines a co-fibred category, and even co-split, over the category of topological spaces, which one calls the co-fibred category of presheaves (resp. sheaves) with values in \(\mathscr{C}\) (understood: over variable spaces). Making explicit the construction of 8, one sees that a morphism from a presheaf \(B\) on \(T\) to a presheaf \(A\) on \(S\) is a pair \((f,u)\) formed of a continuous map from \(T\) to \(S\), and of a morphism \(u\colon A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} f_*(B)\) in the category \(\mathscr{P}(S)\). This description holds equally for morphisms of sheaves, \(\mathscr{F}\) being a full subcategory of \(\mathscr{P}\).

    In the most important cases, the category \(\mathscr{P}\) and the category \(\mathscr{F}\) over \(\mathscr{E}\) are also fibred categories, i.e. for every continuous map, the direct image functors \(\mathscr{P}(T)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{P}(S)\) and \(\mathscr{F}(T)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{F}(S)\) have an adjoint functor, which is then denoted \(f^*\) and called the inverse image functor of presheaves resp. the inverse image functor of sheaves, by the continuous map \(f\). This functor exists for example if \(\mathscr{C}=\mathbf{Ens}\). One can show that the functor \(f^*\colon \mathscr{P}(S)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{P}(T)\) exists whenever in \(\mathscr{C}\) the direct limits (relative to diagrams in the Universe considered) exist. The question is less easy for \(\mathscr{F}\); one will note indeed that (even in the case \(\mathscr{C}=\mathbf{Ens}\)) the inverse image of a presheaf which is a sheaf is in general not a sheaf, in other words the inverse image functor of sheaves is not isomorphic to the functor induced by the inverse image functor of presheaves (despite the common notation \(f^*\)). Thus, \(\mathscr{F}\) is a co-fibred subcategory of \(\mathscr{P}\), but not a fibred subcategory, i.e. the inclusion functor \(\mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{P}\) is not fibrant.

    The co-fibred category \(\mathscr{P}\) can be deduced from a more general co-fibred (or rather fibred) category, obtained as follows. For every category \(\mathscr{U}\) (in the fixed Universe), one sets

    \[ \mathscr{P}(\mathscr{U})=\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}(\mathscr{U},\mathscr{C}) \]

    and one notes that \(\mathscr{U}\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}\mathscr{P}(\mathscr{U})\) is in a natural way a contravariant functor in \(\mathscr{U}\), from the category \(\mathbf{Cat}\) to \(\mathbf{Cat}\). It thus defines a split category over \(\mathscr{E}=\mathbf{Cat}\), which we shall denote \(\mathbf{Cat}_{/\!/\mathscr{C}}\). The objects of this category are the pairs \((\mathscr{U},p)\) of a category \(\mathscr{U}\) and a functor \(p\colon\mathscr{U}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{C}\), and a morphism from \((\mathscr{U},p)\) to \((\mathscr{V},q)\) is essentially a pair \((f,u)\), where \(f\) is a functor \(\mathscr{U}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{V}\) and \(u\) a homomorphism of functors \(u\colon p\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} qf\). We leave to the reader the task of making explicit the composition of morphisms in \(\mathbf{Cat}_{/\!/\mathscr{C}}\). The projection-functor

    \[ \mathscr{F}=\mathbf{Cat}_{/\!/\mathscr{C}}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{E}=\mathbf{Cat} \]

    associates to the pair \((\mathscr{U},p)\) the object \(\mathscr{U}\); the fibre-category at \(\mathscr{U}\) is the category \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}(\mathscr{U},\mathscr{C})\) (up to isomorphism). When in \(\mathscr{C}\) the direct limits exist, one shows easily that the fibred category \(\mathbf{Cat}_{/\!/\mathscr{C}}\) over \(\mathbf{Cat}\) is also co-fibred over \(\mathbf{Cat}\), i.e. one can define the notion of direct image of a functor \(p\colon\mathscr{U}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{C}\) by a functor \(f\colon\mathscr{U}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{V}\). The category of presheaves is deduced from the preceding fibred category by the base change

    \[ \mathbf{Top}^\circ\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathbf{Cat} \]

    (functor \(S\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}\mathscr{U}(S)\) defined above), which gives a fibred category over \(\mathbf{Top}^\circ\), and passing to the opposite category, one obtains the co-fibred category \(\mathscr{P}\) of presheaves over \(\mathbf{Top}\). The notion of inverse image of a functor corresponds to that of direct image of a presheaf, the notion of direct image of a functor to that of inverse image of a presheaf.

  3. c)

    Objects with operators over an object with operators

    Let \(\mathscr{F}\) be a category over \(\mathscr{E}\), and let \(S\) be an object of \(\mathscr{E}\) on which a group \(G\) operates, on the left to fix ideas. This object with operators can be interpreted as corresponding to a functor \(\lambda\colon\mathscr{E}'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{E}\) from the category (with a single object, having \(G\) as group of endomorphisms) \(\mathscr{E}'\) defined by \(G\), to the category \(\mathscr{E}\), and thus defines by base change a category \(\mathscr{F}'\) over \(\mathscr{E}'\), which is fibred resp. co-fibred when \(\mathscr{F}\) is so over \(\mathscr{E}\). A section of \(\mathscr{E}'\) over \(\mathscr{F}'\) (necessarily cartesian, since \(\mathscr{E}'\) is a groupoid, and every isomorphism in \(\mathscr{F}'\) is cartesian by virtue of 12) can also be interpreted as an \(\mathscr{E}\)-functor \(\mathscr{E}'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{F}\) over \(\lambda\), or also as an object with operators \(\xi\) in \(\mathscr{F}\) “over” the object with operators \(S\).

  4. d)

    Pairs of quasi-inverse adjoint functors; self-dualities

    When the base-category \(\mathscr{E}\) is reduced to two objects \(a,b\) and, besides the identity arrows, to two isomorphisms \(f\colon a\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} b\) and \(g\colon b\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} a\) inverse to one another (i.e. \(\mathscr{E}\) is a connected rigid groupoid with two objects), a normalized cleft category over \(\mathscr{E}\) is essentially the same thing as the system formed by two categories \(\mathscr{F}_a\) and \(\mathscr{F}_b\) and a pair of adjoint functors \(G\colon \mathscr{F}_a\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{F}_b\) and \(F\colon \mathscr{F}_b\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{F}_a\), which are equivalences of categories (hence quasi-inverse to one another). One takes for \(\mathscr{F}_a\) and \(\mathscr{F}_b\) the fibre categories of \(\mathscr{F}\), for \(F\) and \(G\) the functors \(f^*\) and \(g^*\), and the two isomorphisms

    \[ u\colon FG\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow}\mathrm{id}_{\mathscr{F}_a}\qquad v\colon GF\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow}\mathrm{id}_{\mathscr{F}_b} \]

    are \(c_{g,f}\) and \(c_{f,g}\). The two usual conditions of compatibility between \(u\) and \(v\) are none other than condition 4 \(B)\) for the composites \(fgf\) and \(gfg\). It is easy to show that these conditions suffice to imply that one indeed has a pseudo-functor \(\mathscr{E}^\circ\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathbf{Cat}\).

    An interesting case is that in which one has

    \[ \mathscr{F}_b=\mathscr{F}_a^\circ,\quad G=F^\circ,\quad v=u^\circ. \]

    One calls self-duality in a category \(\mathscr{C}\) the datum of a functor \(D\colon \mathscr{C}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{C}^\circ\), and of an isomorphism \(u\colon DD^\circ\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow}\mathrm{id}_{\mathscr{C}}\), such that \(u\) and the isomorphism \(u^\circ\colon D^\circ D\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow}\mathrm{id}_{\mathscr{C}^\circ}\) make \((D,D^\circ)\) a pair of adjoint functors, (necessarily quasi-inverse to one another). This condition is written:

    \[ D(u(x))=u(D(x))\qquad \text{for every $x\in\operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{C})$.} \]
  5. e)

    Categories over a discrete category \(\mathscr{E}\). One says that \(\mathscr{E}\) is a discrete category if every arrow in it is an identity arrow, so that \(\mathscr{E}\) is defined up to unique isomorphism by the knowledge of the set \(I=\operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{E})\). The datum of a category \(\mathscr{F}\) over \(\mathscr{E}\) thus amounts (up to unique isomorphism) to the datum of a family of categories \(\mathscr{F}_i\) (\(i\in I\)), the fibre categories. Every category \(\mathscr{F}\) over \(\mathscr{E}\) is fibred, every \(\mathscr{E}\)-functor \(\mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{G}\) is cartesian, one has a canonical isomorphism

    \[ \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}/-}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G}) \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \underset{i}\prod\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}(\mathscr{F}_i,\mathscr{G}_i). \]

    In particular, one obtains

    \[ \mathbf{\Gamma}(\mathscr{F}/\mathscr{E})=\varprojlim\mathscr{F}/\mathscr{E}\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow}\underset{i}\prod \mathscr{E}_i. \]
  6. f)

    Suppose that \(\mathscr{E}\) has exactly two objects \(S\) and \(T\), and, besides the identity morphisms, one morphism \(f\colon T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\). Then a category \(\mathscr{F}\) over \(\mathscr{E}\) is defined, up to unique \(\mathscr{E}\)-isomorphism, by the datum of two categories \(\mathscr{F}_S\) and \(\mathscr{F}_T\) and of a bifunctor \(H(\eta,\xi)\) on \(\mathscr{F}_T^\circ\times\mathscr{F}_S\), with values in \(\mathbf{Ens}\). Indeed, if \(\mathscr{F}\) is a category over \(\mathscr{E}\), to it one associates the two fibre-categories \(\mathscr{F}_S\) and \(\mathscr{F}_T\) and the bifunctor \(H(\eta,\xi)=\operatorname{Hom}_f(\eta,\xi)\). We leave to the reader the task of making explicit the construction in the reverse direction. For the category envisaged to be fibred (or prefibred, this comes to the same thing) it is necessary and sufficient that the functor \(H\) be representable with respect to the argument \(\xi\). For it to be co-fibred, it is necessary and sufficient that \(H\) be representable with respect to the argument \(\eta\).

  7. g)

    Let \(\mathscr{F}=\mathscr{C}\times\mathscr{E}\), considered as a category over \(\mathscr{E}\) by means of \(\mathrm{pr}_2\). Then \(\mathscr{F}\) is fibred and co-fibred over \(\mathscr{E}\), and is even endowed with a canonical splitting and co-splitting, corresponding to the constant functor on \(\mathscr{E}\), resp. on \(\mathscr{E}^\circ\), with values in \(\mathbf{Cat}\), of value \(\mathscr{C}\). One has

    \[ \mathbf{\Gamma}(\mathscr{F}/\mathscr{E})\simeq\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}(\mathscr{E},\mathscr{C}) \]

    and \(\varprojlim\mathscr{F}/\mathscr{E}\) corresponds to the full subcategory formed of the functors \(F\colon\mathscr{E\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{C}}\) transforming arbitrary morphisms into isomorphisms.

pages.title: Functors on a cleaved category
pages.html:
12 Functors on a cleaved category

Let \(\mathscr{F}\) be a normalized cleaved category over \(\mathscr{E}\). For every object \(S\) of \(\mathscr{E}\) we denote by

\[ i_S\colon \mathscr{F}_S\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{F} \]

the inclusion functor. We thus have a functorial homomorphism, for every morphism \(f\colon T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) in \(\mathscr{E}\):

\[ \alpha_f\colon i_T f^*\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} i_S, \]

where \(f^*\) is the base-change functor \(\mathscr{F}_S\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{F}_T\) for \(f\) defined by the cleavage. Now let

\[ F\colon\mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{C} \]

be a functor from \(\mathscr{F}\) into a category \(\mathscr{C}\); we set, for every \(S\in\operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{E})\),

\[ F_S=F\circ i_S\colon\mathscr{F}_S\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{C} \]

and for every \(f\colon T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) in \(\mathscr{E}\),

\[ \varphi_f=F*\alpha_f\colon F_T f^*\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F_S \]

Thus, to every functor \(F\colon \mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{C}\), there is associated a family \((F_S)\) of functors \(\mathscr{F}_S\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{C}\), and a family \((\varphi_f)\) of homomorphisms of functors \(F_T f^*\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F_S\). These families satisfy the following conditions:

a) \(\varphi_{\mathrm{id}_S}=\mathrm{id}_{F_S}\).

b) For two morphisms \(f\colon T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) and \(g\colon U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T\) in \(\mathscr{E}\), we have commutativity in the square of functorial homomorphisms:

\[ \xymatrix@C=2cm{{F_U g^* f^*}\ar[r]^-{F_U*c_{f,g}}\ar[d]_{\varphi_g * f^*}&{F_U(fg)^*}\ar[d]^{\varphi_{fg}}\\{F_T f^*}\ar[r]^-{\varphi_f}&{F_S.}} \]

The first relation is trivial, and the second relation is obtained by applying the functor \(F\) to the commutative diagram

\[ \xymatrix@C=2cm{{g^* f^* (\xi)}\ar[r]^-{c_{f,g}(\xi)}\ar[d]_{\alpha_g(f^*(\xi))}&{(fg)^*(\xi)}\ar[d]^{\alpha_{fg}(\xi)}\\{f^*(\xi)}\ar[r]^-{\alpha_f(\xi)}&{\xi}} \]

for a variable object \(\xi\) in \(\mathscr{F}_S\).

If \(G\) is a second functor \(\mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{C}\), giving rise to functors \(G_S\colon\mathscr{F}_S\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{C}\) and functorial homomorphisms \(\psi_f\colon G_Tf\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G_S\), and if \(u\colon F\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G\) is a functorial homomorphism, then there correspond to it functorial homomorphisms \(u*i_S\):

\[ u_S\colon F_S\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G_S \]

and one verifies at once that for every morphism \(f\colon T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) in \(\mathscr{E}\), we have commutativity in the squares

\[ \begin{array}{c} \xymatrix@C=1.5cm{{F_Tf^*}\ar[r]^-{\phi_f}\ar[d]_{u_T * f^*}&{F_S}\ar[d]^{u_S}\\{G_T f^*}\ar[r]^-{\psi_f}&{G_S}} \end{array} \tag*{c)} \]
Proposition 1

Let \(\mathscr{H}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{C})\) be the category whose objects are the pairs consisting of families \((F_S)\) (\(S\in\operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{F})\)) of functors \(\mathscr{F}_S\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{C}\), and of families \((\varphi_f)\) (\(f\in\operatorname{Fl}(\mathscr{F})\)) of functorial homomorphisms \(F_Tf^*\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F_S\), satisfying the conditions a) and b), and where the morphisms are the families \((u_S)\) (\(S\in\operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{F})\)) of homomorphisms \(F_S\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G_S\), satisfying the commutativity condition c) written above, (the composition of morphisms being performed by the composition of the homomorphisms of functors \(\mathscr{F}_S\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{C}\)). Then the two laws made explicit above define an isomorphism \(K\) of the category \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{C})\) with the category \(\mathscr{H}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{C})\).

It is trivial that we do indeed have here a functor from the first category into the second. This functor is fully faithful, for, given \(F,G\), the map \(\operatorname{Hom}(F,G)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Hom}(K(F),K(G))\) is trivially injective; to show that it is surjective, it suffices to note that the commutativity condition c) expresses the functoriality of the maps \(u(\xi)=u_S(\xi)\colon F(S)=F_S(\xi)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G(\xi)=G_S(\xi)\) for the homomorphisms of the form \(\alpha_f(\xi)\) in \(\mathscr{F}\); on the other hand, we have functoriality on each fibre category, i.e. for the morphisms in \(\mathscr{F}\) that are \(T\)-morphisms (\(T\in\operatorname{Ob}(\mathscr{E})\)), whence functoriality for every morphism in \(\mathscr{F}\), since an \(f\)-morphism (where \(f\colon T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is a morphism in \(\mathscr{E}\) is in a unique way) a composite of a morphism \(\alpha_f(\xi)\) and a \(T\)-morphism. It thus remains to prove that the functor \(K\) is bijective on objects. The preceding argument already shows that \(K\) is injective on objects; it remains to prove that it is surjective, i.e. that if one starts from a system \((F_S),(\varphi_f)\), satisfying a) and b); and if one defines a map \(\operatorname{Ob}\mathscr{F}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Ob}\mathscr{C}\) by

\[ F(\xi)=F_S(\xi) \quad\text{for } \xi\in\operatorname{Ob}\mathscr{F}_S\subset\operatorname{Ob}\mathscr{F} \]

and a map \(\operatorname{Fl}(\mathscr{F})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Fl}(\mathscr{C})\) by

\[ F(\alpha_f(\xi)u')=\varphi_f(\xi)\,F_T(u') \]

for every morphism \(f\colon T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) in \(\mathscr{E}\), every object \(\xi\) of \(\mathscr{F}_S\) and every \(T\)-morphism \(u'\) with target \(f^*(\xi)\), then one obtains a functor \(F\) from \(\mathscr{F}\) into \(\mathscr{C}\). Indeed, the relation \(F(\mathrm{id}_\xi)=\mathrm{id}_{F(\xi)}\) is trivial, and it remains to prove the multiplicativity \(F(uv)=F(u)F(v)\) when we have an \(f\)-morphism \(u\colon\eta\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\xi\) and a \(g\)-morphism \(v\colon\zeta\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\nu\), with \(f\colon T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) and \(g\colon U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T\) morphisms of \(\mathscr{E}\). Setting \(w=uv\), we shall have

\[ u=\alpha_f(\xi)u',\quad v=\alpha_g(\eta)v',\quad w=\alpha_{fg}(\xi)w' \]

with

\[ w'=c_{f,g}(\xi)g^*(u')v'\quad \hbox{(cf. Nº 8)}. \]

With these notations, we must prove the commutativity of the outer contour of the diagram below:

\[ \xymatrix{ F_U(\zeta) \ar@{-<} `u[r] `[rrrrrr]^{F(w')} [rrrrrr] \ar[rr]^{F_u(v')} \ar[drr]_{F(v)} & & F_Ug^*(\eta) \ar[rr]^{F_ug^*(u')} \ar[d]^{\varphi_g(\eta)} & & F_Ug^*f^*(\xi) \ar[rr]^{F_U(c_{f,g}(\xi))}\ar[d]^{\varphi_g(f^*(\xi))}& & F_U(fg)^*(\xi) \ar[d]^{\varphi_{fg}(\xi)} \\ & & F_T(\eta) \ar@{-<} `d[r] `[rrrr]_{F(u)} [rrrr] \ar[rr]_{F_T(u')} & & F_Tf^*(\xi) \ar[rr]_{\varphi_f(\xi)} & & F_S(\xi) } \]

Now the left-hand triangle is commutative by definition of \(F(v)\), the middle square is commutative since it is deduced from the homomorphism \(u'\colon \xi\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} f(\eta)\) by the functorial homomorphism \(\alpha_g\), and finally the right-hand square is commutative by virtue of condition b). The desired conclusion follows.

Suppose now that \(\mathscr{C}\) is likewise a normalized cleaved category over \(\mathscr{E}\), which we shall henceforth call \(\mathscr{G}\), and that we are interested in the \(\mathscr{E}\)-functors from \(\mathscr{F}\) into \(\mathscr{G}\). If \(F\) is such a functor, it induces functors

\[ F_S\colon \mathscr{F}_S\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{G}_S \]

for the fibre categories. On the other hand, for every morphism \(f\colon T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) in \(\mathscr{E}\), and every object \(\xi\) in \(\mathscr{F}_S\), the \(f\)-morphism \(F(\alpha_f(\xi))\) factors in a unique way through a \(T\)-morphism

\[ \varphi_f(\xi)\colon F_T(f^*_{\mathscr{F}}(\xi))\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} f^*_{\mathscr{G}}(F_S(\xi)) \]

(where the \(\mathscr{F}\) or the \(\mathscr{G}\) as a subscript indicates the cleaved category for which one takes the inverse image functor), whence a functorial homomorphism of functors from \(\mathscr{F}_S\) into \(\mathscr{G}_T\):

\[ \varphi_f\colon F_T f^*_{\mathscr{F}}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} f^*_{\mathscr{G}}F_S. \]

The two systems \((F_S)\) and \((\varphi_f)\) satisfy the following conditions:

a') \(\varphi_{\mathrm{id}_S}=\mathrm{id}_{F_S}\).

b') For two morphisms \(f\colon T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) and \(g\colon U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T\) in \(\mathscr{E}\), we have commutativity in the following diagram of functorial homomorphisms:

\[ \xymatrix{{F_Ug_F^*f_F^*}\ar[rr]^{F_U *c_{f,g}^\mathscr{F}}\ar[d]_{\varphi_{g^*}f^*_{\mathscr{F}}}& &{F_U(fg)^*_F}\ar[dd]^{\varphi_{fg}}\\{g^*_{\mathscr{G}}F_Tf^*_F}\ar[d]_{g^*_{\mathscr{G}}*\varphi_f}& & \\{g^*_{\mathscr{G}}f^*_{\mathscr{G}}F_S}\ar[rr]^{c_{f,g}^\mathscr{G}*F_S}& &{(fg)^*_{\mathscr{G}}F_S.}} \]

We leave the verification to the reader, as well as the statement and proof of the analogue of proposition 1, implying that one obtains in this way a one-to-one correspondence between the set of \(\mathscr{E}\)-functors from \(\mathscr{F}\) to \(\mathscr{G}\), and the set of systems \((F_S)\),\((\varphi_f)\) satisfying conditions a') and b') above. Of course, in this correspondence, the cartesian functors are characterized by the property that the homomorphisms \(\varphi_f\) are isomorphisms.

Remark

Of course, it is most often preferable to reason directly on fibred categories without using explicit cleavages, which in particular dispenses with appealing, for the simple notion of \(\mathscr{E}\)-functor or of cartesian \(\mathscr{E}\)-functor, to a cumbersome interpretation as above. It is in order to avoid unbearable heaviness, and in order to obtain more intrinsic statements, that we have had to give up starting, as in [2], from the notion of cloven category (called “fibred category” in loc. cit. ), which passes into second place in favour of that of fibred category. It is moreover probable that, contrary to the usage still predominant now, tied to old habits of thought, it will end up proving more convenient, in universal problems, not to place the emphasis on one solution supposed to be chosen once and for all, but to place all solutions on an equal footing.

[1]
A. Grothendieck, Sur quelques points d'algèbre homologique, Tôhoku Math. J. 9 (1957) p. 119–221. ↩
[2]
A. Grothendieck, Technique de descente et Théorèmes d'existence, I, Séminaire Bourbaki 190, décembre 1959. ↩

VII: does not exist
